source: all-codex xhigh fleet (15 topics) + codex cross-critique; consolidated Opus 4.8

lenses.name: Async Workflows
lenses.focus: Owns long-running operation contracts by replacing timeout-prone synchronous calls with explicit job, callback, or saga-facing APIs.
concerns: 202 Accepted, job resources, polling, callbacks, and webhook tradeoffs
operation state machine, terminal states, cancellation, and expiration
progress reporting, partial results, and recoverable failure details
idempotent submission and duplicate job detection
webhook signing, retry, ordering, and dead-letter behavior
client responsibility when completion arrives before acknowledgement

signals: requests may exceed gateway, browser, or client timeout limits
operations involve external systems or human approval
clients need progress, cancellation, or eventual completion callbacks
same workflow is currently exposed through ad hoc status endpoints
webhooks or background jobs are being added without a unified contract

lenses.tension: Async contracts make reliability explicit but add client orchestration cost compared with one blocking call.

lenses.name: Authorization Surface
lenses.focus: Owns how identity, scopes, tenant boundaries, and redaction rules appear in the API contract, not only in backend middleware.
concerns: scope and permission vocabulary aligned with resources and operations
tenant isolation in paths, tokens, page cursors, idempotency keys, and webhooks
field-level redaction and partial response behavior
401 versus 403 versus not-found semantics for sensitive resources
delegated access, service accounts, impersonation, and audit context
authorization examples and negative contract tests

signals: API exposes tenant, organization, workspace, or project boundaries
clients receive different fields depending on permissions
authorization behavior leaks existence of sensitive resources
OAuth scopes, API keys, or service accounts are being redesigned
public contracts need security review before partner exposure

lenses.tension: Making authorization explicit strengthens correctness and auditability but can complicate otherwise simple endpoint and SDK design.

lenses.name: Compatibility Path
lenses.focus: Owns API evolution by forcing every contract change through an explicit compatibility, deprecation, and migration path.
concerns: additive versus breaking schema and behavior changes
version placement in URL, headers, media types, packages, or schemas
field optionality, defaulting, enum expansion, and unknown value handling
deprecation windows, sunset headers, migration guides, and telemetry
server capability negotiation and client feature detection
parallel support cost for old clients and long-lived integrations

signals: public or partner clients cannot be upgraded in lockstep
API schema changes frequently
enum values or response fields are added without client tolerance
a v2 API or migration program is being considered
contracts are consumed by SDKs, generated clients, or external partners

lenses.tension: Backward compatibility protects consumers but can preserve flawed shapes and increase maintenance burden.

lenses.name: API DX
lenses.focus: Owns the consumer experience by treating docs, examples, SDKs, sandbox behavior, and first successful call as part of the contract.
concerns: complete OpenAPI, protobuf, GraphQL, or AsyncAPI source of truth
copy-pasteable examples for auth, pagination, retries, and errors
SDK ergonomics, generated-client escape hatches, and naming consistency
sandbox data, test credentials, and deterministic examples
client-side defaults for timeouts, retries, idempotency, and rate limits
documentation drift detection and release-note quality

signals: external developers or many internal teams consume the API
integration support load is high
SDKs or generated clients are part of the product surface
docs omit failure, pagination, auth, or retry examples
time-to-first-successful-call matters commercially or operationally

lenses.tension: DX favors simple, memorable surfaces and helpful defaults, which can hide necessary protocol nuance or backend constraints.

lenses.name: Error Semantics
lenses.focus: Owns the error contract as a machine-actionable recovery interface, not a collection of messages for humans to read.
concerns: stable error codes distinct from localized or diagnostic text
retryable versus terminal failures and safe retry guidance
validation error structure with field paths and constraint identifiers
HTTP, RPC status, or stream-close mapping without semantic loss
correlation IDs, incident references, and support-safe diagnostics
privacy boundaries for errors returned to untrusted clients

signals: clients branch on error strings
support incidents require correlating client failures with server logs
API has validation-heavy requests or partial failures
multiple transports must expose the same failure semantics
retry logic is ambiguous or duplicated in clients

lenses.tension: A strict error taxonomy improves automation but can become governance overhead if every edge case needs a named public code.

lenses.name: Retry Safety
lenses.focus: Owns mutation safety by specifying exactly which operations may be retried, deduplicated, replayed, or rejected.
concerns: idempotency key scope, entropy, tenant binding, and expiration window
deduplication storage, response replay, and in-flight request races
side effect boundaries for payments, emails, provisioning, and webhooks
client timeout behavior when the server may still commit
safe retry status codes, conflict responses, and duplicate detection
idempotency across batch, async, and streaming write paths

signals: API performs non-reversible mutations
clients run on unreliable networks or mobile connections
payment, provisioning, notification, or external side effects are involved
timeouts leave clients unsure whether a mutation committed
retry middleware is being introduced at clients, gateways, or workers

lenses.tension: Strong retry safety requires persistent deduplication and clearer side-effect boundaries, which can slow simple mutation delivery.

lenses.name: Pagination Semantics
lenses.focus: Owns list and search contracts so pagination, ordering, filtering, and continuation behavior remain correct under real data churn.
concerns: cursor versus offset pagination and when offset is unacceptable
stable sort keys, deterministic tie-breakers, and missing-order hazards
snapshot consistency, read-after-write expectations, and duplicate or skipped rows
filter grammar, indexability, and server-enforced limits
page token opacity, expiration, replay safety, and tenant scoping
total count semantics and cost disclosure

signals: API exposes list, search, export, sync, or infinite-scroll endpoints
underlying data changes while clients paginate
large tenants or high-cardinality collections are expected
clients need resumable traversal or incremental sync
current pagination uses offset with unstable ordering

lenses.tension: Correct cursor and snapshot semantics add backend complexity and may constrain flexible ad hoc querying.

lenses.name: Protocol Fit
lenses.focus: Owns the REST, RPC, streaming, webhook, or event choice by matching the interface to the interaction shape instead of defaulting to a fashionable protocol.
concerns: resource lifecycle operations versus command-style actions
latency sensitivity and request-response coupling
client fan-out, server fan-out, and subscription cardinality
human-debuggable HTTP semantics versus typed binary contracts
network intermediaries, caching, gateways, and load balancers
operational maturity required for bidirectional or long-lived connections

signals: team is debating REST versus RPC versus streaming
API includes both CRUD resources and command-heavy workflows
clients need low-latency updates or server push
existing endpoint style is inconsistent across product areas
gateway, proxy, or platform constraints affect the protocol choice

lenses.tension: Optimizing each interaction for its best protocol can fragment the platform and make DX, documentation, and governance harder.

lenses.name: Quota Contract
lenses.focus: Owns throttling and quota semantics so clients can shape traffic predictably instead of discovering platform limits through failures.
concerns: rate limit dimensions by tenant, token, endpoint, method, cost, or burst
standard headers or metadata for remaining budget and reset time
429, overload, and quota-exhausted error distinctions
client backoff guidance, retry-after precision, and clock-skew tolerance
bulk, pagination, export, and streaming cost models
plan-specific limits and fairness across multi-tenant workloads

signals: API serves many tenants or third-party integrations
traffic spikes or client loops can degrade shared infrastructure
expensive list, export, search, or AI-like operations exist
rate limits are undocumented or enforced inconsistently
clients need to schedule work within known quotas

lenses.tension: Explicit quota contracts improve fairness but expose platform policy and may constrain product packaging flexibility.

lenses.name: Resource Boundaries
lenses.focus: Owns the public API shape by making resources, commands, and workflow boundaries explicit before endpoints or methods multiply.
concerns: stable resource identities and canonical URLs or method names
operation granularity that avoids chatty clients and overloaded endpoints
separation of query models from mutation commands
domain vocabulary consistency across endpoints, SDKs, and docs
bulk operations, partial updates, and command validation boundaries
internal model leakage through public request and response shapes

signals: API surface has many ad hoc endpoints
clients must call several endpoints to complete one user action
request or response names expose database tables or internal services
bulk, patch, or command endpoints are being added
teams disagree about noun-oriented versus verb-oriented API design

lenses.tension: A clean conceptual model can slow delivery when the immediate need is a narrow integration or tactical endpoint.

lenses.name: Streaming Flow
lenses.focus: Owns streaming contracts by making ordering, delivery, resume, backpressure, and termination semantics explicit before clients depend on them.
concerns: SSE, WebSocket, gRPC streaming, message bus, or polling fallback choice
ordering guarantees per stream, partition, tenant, or resource
acknowledgement, checkpoint, replay, and resume-token semantics
backpressure, buffering, heartbeat, idle timeout, and disconnect behavior
at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once claims
schema evolution for long-lived streams and old subscribers

signals: clients need live updates or incremental data feeds
polling causes load or unacceptable latency
events must be replayed after disconnects
delivery ordering or duplicate handling affects correctness
long-lived connections cross gateways, mobile networks, or proxies

lenses.tension: Streaming reduces latency and polling waste but introduces operational complexity, stateful connections, and harder client recovery.

lenses.name: Backfill Cutover
lenses.focus: Design historical backfill and incremental catch-up as one restartable protocol with a clear cutover point.
concerns: chunking, throttling, checkpointing, and restart behavior for large historical scans
snapshot high-water mark capture and catch-up from that mark without gaps
dual-read, dual-write, shadow-write, or compare-only phases before cutover
diff validation using counts, checksums, sampling, and domain invariants
rollback, pause, resume, and re-run procedures for failed migrations

signals: a new projection, warehouse, index, service, or sync target needs historical data
large-table migration must happen while writes continue
an outage or bug requires replaying a range without duplicating effects
teams need evidence that old and new stores match before switching traffic

lenses.tension: Temporary pipelines and validation phases consume operational capacity but avoid blind cutovers and irreversible drift.

lenses.name: Business Conflict Policy
lenses.focus: Resolve concurrent or contradictory facts with explicit domain policy instead of defaulting to last-writer-wins.
concerns: record-level versus field-level conflict detection and resolution
source priority, actor authority, and business-critical invariant precedence
version vectors, compare-and-swap tokens, or conflict facts where simple timestamps are insufficient
manual review queues and user-facing conflict surfacing for non-automatable cases
audit trail of discarded, overridden, and merged values

signals: multiple systems, users, or devices can update the same entity
offline edits, delayed events, or bidirectional sync are in scope
last-writer-wins would lose legally, financially, or operationally important data
product requirements mention override, approval, review, or disputed records

lenses.tension: Forces product and domain choices early; can slow teams that hoped conflict handling was just a storage concern.

lenses.name: CDC Watermark Contracts
lenses.focus: Define cursor and watermark semantics as a durable contract so every replay, resume, and lag calculation is explainable.
concerns: source ordering primitive choice: log sequence, commit timestamp, version vector, or synthetic sequence
per-stream and per-partition watermark persistence with explicit inclusive or exclusive bounds
snapshot-to-stream handoff and high-water mark capture
gap detection, rewind policy, and duplicate-delivery tolerance
lag, staleness, and cursor provenance exposed to operators

signals: design mentions CDC, polling, sync cursors, resume tokens, or stream offsets
lost updates or duplicate events appear after restarts or deploys
multiple partitions or sources need a single notion of progress
teams need auditability for what was processed up to a point

lenses.tension: Adds durable state and operational ceremony; can slow prototypes that only need a one-shot import.

lenses.name: Consistency Auditability
lenses.focus: Expose divergence, reconciliation outcomes, and data lineage as operational health signals, not occasional manual checks.
concerns: drift detectors using counts, checksums, domain invariants, and sampled record comparisons
watermark lag, conflict rate, tombstone propagation, and retry exhaustion metrics
reconciliation reports that explain changed, skipped, failed, and repaired records
per-record lineage from source fact through transform to materialized target
audit controls for who changed conflict, linkage, or repair decisions

signals: support tickets report mismatched records but engineers cannot localize drift
migration or sync success is currently based on absence of errors
regulated domains require traceability for corrected or discarded data
operators need to decide whether to pause, rewind, replay, or repair

lenses.tension: Instrumentation can add storage, background load, and sensitive lineage data; it only pays off if teams will act on the signals.

lenses.name: Idempotent Reconciliation
lenses.focus: Make reconciliation a deterministic, total, idempotent function before optimizing transport or storage.
concerns: pure comparison of local, remote, and prior-sync state into a finite action set
retry safety for upserts, deletes, link changes, and side-effect scheduling
commutative and associative merge properties where inputs can arrive in different orders
clear distinction between null, missing, unknown, and intentionally cleared values
property tests for duplicate input, reordered input, and replayed input

signals: running the same sync twice changes the result
retry logic creates duplicate records or duplicate side effects
merge behavior lives in scattered imperative branches
the system must support replay, repair, or at-least-once delivery

lenses.tension: A rigorous reconciliation model can reject convenient one-off patches and may expose unresolved domain decisions.

lenses.name: Identity Linkage
lenses.focus: Own entity identity, linkage, dedupe, split, merge, and field survivorship as a data product boundary.
concerns: natural keys, surrogate keys, external IDs, and mapping table ownership
deterministic matching versus probabilistic record linkage with confidence thresholds
split and merge history that keeps old references resolvable
golden-record survivorship rules per field and per source
PII exposure, consent constraints, and auditability of linkage decisions

signals: duplicate customers, accounts, products, people, or locations appear across sources
imports rely on fuzzy matching, mutable natural keys, or external IDs
downstream systems disagree on the canonical entity identifier
business users need merge, unmerge, or survivorship controls

lenses.tension: Adds probabilistic decisions and review workflows; unnecessary when authoritative immutable IDs already exist.

lenses.name: Ordering And Causality
lenses.focus: Make ordering guarantees explicit by entity and partition so causality is preserved without pretending global order exists.
concerns: partition key choice and its relationship to entity identity and contention
per-entity sequence numbers, source versions, and gap detection
out-of-order buffering, late event handling, and maximum tolerated lateness
causal dependencies across entities, parent-child records, and link tables
clock skew risks when timestamps are used as ordering evidence

signals: events arrive out of order or from multiple shards
derived state depends on create-before-update or parent-before-child sequences
global ordering is becoming a throughput bottleneck
identity changes, merges, or repartitioning may move records between streams

lenses.tension: Causal buffering and per-entity ordering protect correctness but add latency and can constrain sharding strategy.

lenses.name: Projection Contracts
lenses.focus: Treat caches, indexes, denormalized tables, and read models as rebuildable projections with explicit source, version, and freshness contracts.
concerns: source-of-truth declaration for each derived field and aggregate view
projection versioning and rebuild procedures for schema or rule changes
freshness guarantees visible to API, UI, and operator decisions
invalidation, compaction, and repair behavior for partial projection failures
drift prevention for denormalized columns, search indexes, and materialized views

signals: the design adds caches, search indexes, read models, or denormalized tables
users see stale or contradictory values across screens or APIs
teams cannot rebuild derived state from a trusted source
read performance work risks duplicating business rules into storage

lenses.tension: Projection discipline adds rebuild and versioning work; inline transactional updates may be simpler for small, strongly consistent models.

lenses.name: Replayable Schema Evolution
lenses.focus: Keep historic events, snapshots, and records readable and semantically mappable after the data model changes.
concerns: versioned payloads, envelopes, and semantic compatibility rules
defaults, transforms, and migration paths for missing or renamed fields
lineage metadata that records source version, mapping version, and migration time
replay test corpora that include old, malformed, and boundary-case records
deprecation windows and cleanup criteria for legacy readers and writers

signals: events or CDC logs may be replayed months or years later
schema changes must coexist with old producers, consumers, or backfills
teams plan to rename, split, merge, or reinterpret fields
migration failures would require rebuilding derived state from history

lenses.tension: Carrying compatibility code and lineage metadata delays cleanup but prevents replay from becoming a hidden breaking change.

lenses.name: Consistency Budget
lenses.focus: Classify each invariant and read path by the strongest consistency guarantee it truly needs and can afford.
concerns: hard invariants that require transactions, locks, constraints, leases, or consensus
staleness budgets, monotonic reads, read-your-writes, and session guarantees
user-visible stale states and the copy required to explain or hide them
compensating actions for invariant violations discovered after async processing
availability, latency, and throughput costs of stronger guarantees

signals: requirements use phrases like must never, immediately visible, or eventually consistent
cache, replica, projection, or search freshness is part of user behavior
teams are debating synchronous calls versus async events
financial, inventory, access-control, or compliance invariants are at stake

lenses.tension: Strong guarantees can reduce availability and throughput; eventual guarantees create product, repair, and observability obligations.

lenses.name: Tombstone Delete Semantics
lenses.focus: Model deletion as a first-class state transition so downstream systems neither resurrect nor silently forget records.
concerns: soft delete, hard delete, purge, and undelete states with explicit transitions
tombstone retention windows versus log compaction and replay needs
delete ordering relative to updates, link changes, and projection rebuilds
cascade behavior for dependents, joins, search indexes, and caches
privacy erasure requirements separated from operational tombstones

signals: records reappear after backfill, replay, or cache rebuild
deletes must propagate across services, indexes, warehouses, or devices
the system mixes soft delete, archive, and legal erase flows
foreign-key-like relationships exist outside one database transaction

lenses.tension: Preserving delete history helps correctness and auditability but can conflict with minimization, compaction, and simple query models.

lenses.name: Transactional Boundaries
lenses.focus: Choose the atomic boundary for state changes, emitted facts, and consumed cursors before designing workers or APIs.
concerns: same-transaction persistence for domain state, outbox records, and local cursors where required
inbox, dedupe, and idempotency-key tables for consumed messages or remote effects
batch commit size, isolation level, and lock contention tradeoffs
saga and compensation boundaries when one transaction cannot cover all effects
explicit rejection of vague exactly-once claims across independent systems

signals: code publishes messages after commit or advances cursors before side effects finish
a workflow writes to multiple databases, queues, or external APIs
failures leave partially applied changes that require manual repair
the design debates two-phase commit, sagas, or exactly-once processing

lenses.tension: Tight atomic boundaries improve correctness but add schema, worker, and throughput costs that may be excessive for single-store flows.

lenses.name: Abstract Factory Family Coherence
lenses.focus: Use Abstract Factory when several related products must be selected, versioned, and swapped as a compatible family.
concerns: family boundary and whether products must be mutually compatible
factory interface segregation so every family is not forced to implement irrelevant products
matrix growth across platform, backend, tenant, protocol, or theme axes
version compatibility between created objects and external systems
fallback behavior when a family lacks one product
the trap of factory explosion when only one product varies or variants are mostly optional parameters

signals: bugs come from mixing objects from incompatible providers, platforms, protocols, or tenants
a request must swap an entire object family at once
several product interfaces evolve together under one backend or deployment target
configuration selects a provider and all downstream collaborators must match it

lenses.tension: It protects compatibility across products but can freeze a large product matrix into public API and make simple additions expensive.

lenses.name: Port Bridge
lenses.focus: Treats bridges as explicit ports where product-facing abstractions delegate to replaceable mechanisms such as storage, rendering, transport, or execution.
concerns: port ownership and dependency direction
capability-oriented interface design
implementation registration and selection
fallback behavior across implementations
contract tests shared by implementations
avoiding framework details in the abstraction

signals: the same feature must support multiple databases, runtimes, transports, renderers, or cloud providers
business code imports framework-specific classes
implementation choice is environment-dependent
teams want to ship a default implementation but allow replacement
testing needs an in-memory or fake implementation with the same semantics

lenses.tension: Creates a stable extension surface but can freeze the wrong abstraction too early; the trap is designing the port around the first implementation's quirks.

lenses.name: Bridge Variation
lenses.focus: Separates two dimensions of change so abstractions and implementations can evolve independently instead of exploding into subclasses.
concerns: identifying independent variation axes
avoiding inheritance matrices
stable abstraction contracts
implementation plugability
cross-product test strategy
naming that makes the two axes obvious

signals: class or module names combine two concerns such as platform plus feature
new variants require touching many siblings
subclass count is growing as a cross-product
business behavior and technical delivery mechanism change at different rates
tests duplicate the same behavior across many implementation variants

lenses.tension: Improves extensibility but introduces an abstraction seam that must be real; the trap is inventing a bridge before there are two genuine change axes.

lenses.name: Builder Invariant Assembly
lenses.focus: Use Builder when construction is a multi-parameter assembly problem and the design must make invalid or unreadable construction hard.
concerns: mandatory versus optional fields and whether mandatory data can be enforced before build
centralized validation and clear build failure semantics
immutable product design rather than exposing a partially built object
safe defaults, named steps, and readable call sites
builder reuse, thread safety, and stale state between builds
the trap of a mutable fluent bag that hides required fields until runtime

signals: constructors have many positional arguments or boolean flags
valid objects require combinations of optional fields, defaults, and cross-field validation
call sites repeatedly assemble immutable values from request, config, or parsed data
public API needs readable object creation without exploding constructor overloads

lenses.tension: It improves readability and invariant control but adds API surface and may delay errors if staged requirements are not enforced.

lenses.name: Chain Pipeline
lenses.focus: Owns ordered handler pipelines where each step may handle, transform, enrich, reject, or pass a request onward.
concerns: handler contract: consume, transform, pass through, short-circuit, or fail
ordering rules, composition mechanics, defaults, and extension registration
shared context mutation versus immutable request-response flow
error semantics, compensation, partial handling, and observability per step
performance costs from deep chains, repeated parsing, or hidden I/O in handlers
debuggability when control flow is distributed across many small handlers

signals: requests pass through validation, authorization, enrichment, normalization, or routing stages
conditionals select the first capable handler
teams need to add filters or processors without editing a central dispatcher
order-dependent bugs or missing stop conditions are already appearing

lenses.tension: Trades a central conditional block for distributed control flow; the trap is an opaque chain where order is implicit and no one owns the full request outcome.

lenses.name: Command Action Boundary
lenses.focus: Owns reifying user or system actions as explicit objects or messages with validation, execution, retry, audit, and undo semantics.
concerns: command envelope shape, identity, actor, authorization context, idempotency key, and causation metadata
separation between command validation, command handling, and domain mutation
serialization, versioning, replay safety, and compatibility for queued or persisted commands
uniform handling of audit trails, retries, scheduling, batching, and cancellation
undo and compensation semantics, including what can be reversed and what must be compensated
avoidance of command-bus ceremony around trivial local method calls

signals: actions need queueing, scheduling, replay, audit, or undo
the same operation is invoked from UI, API, CLI, and background jobs
side effects need a uniform envelope for authorization, validation, idempotency, or tracing
workflow tests should assert submitted actions instead of internal method calls

lenses.tension: Trades simple method calls for explicit action plumbing; the trap is an anemic command bus where every trivial operation becomes a command.

lenses.name: Composite Guardrails
lenses.focus: Audits composite designs for invariants that keep recursive structures correct, bounded, and understandable.
concerns: parent-child ownership rules
mutation permissions
cycle and depth limits
partial failure behavior in recursive operations
bulk operation atomicity
serialization and persistence shape

signals: nested structures can be edited by users or external inputs
recursive operations may partially fail
tree state is persisted, synchronized, or shared across services
cycles or duplicate ownership would corrupt behavior
large composites may cause performance or transaction problems

lenses.tension: Adds rules that make the model less permissive; the trap is treating composite as just a convenient tree while ignoring ownership, mutation, and failure semantics.

lenses.name: Composite Contract
lenses.focus: Models part-whole structures with one uniform contract so callers can operate on leaves and groups without branching on shape.
concerns: leaf versus container behavior
recursive traversal semantics
operation validity across all node types
cycle prevention
aggregate calculations
stable ordering and identity

signals: callers repeatedly check whether an object is a single item or a group
the domain has nested folders, menus, rules, layouts, tasks, org units, or document sections
operations such as render, price, validate, authorize, or execute should apply recursively
new node types are expected
tree traversal logic is duplicated across features

lenses.tension: Reduces caller branching but pressures every node to support the same operations; the trap is hiding invalid leaf operations behind no-ops or runtime exceptions.

lenses.name: Configuration Driven Object Graph
lenses.focus: Use Abstract Factory and Builder deliberately when configuration chooses providers and assembles object graphs, while rejecting stringly or reflective factory sprawl.
concerns: typed mapping from configuration to factories, builders, and product families
early validation of missing, incompatible, or deprecated configuration values
separation between parsing configuration and constructing live resources
plugin discovery and registration without leaking reflection or string keys everywhere
clear defaults and override precedence across environments
the trap of turning configuration into an untyped service locator

signals: runtime config selects backend, storage, protocol, renderer, tenant, or deployment-specific collaborators
object graphs are assembled from nested config with many optional sections
boot failures are late because construction validates config only after side effects start
plugins or providers are loaded dynamically but must still satisfy product contracts

lenses.tension: It makes runtime assembly explicit and validated but can over-centralize creation and make small feature code depend on bootstrapping machinery.

lenses.name: Pattern Fit Triage
lenses.focus: Decide the smallest creation mechanism that matches the actual axis of variability before introducing a named creational pattern.
concerns: whether construction varies by subtype, product family, parameter set, copied exemplar, or process lifetime
call-site pain from conditionals, telescoping constructors, repeated setup, or hidden globals
whether a constructor, static factory, dependency injection binding, or plain function is sufficient
blast radius of exposing a pattern in public API versus keeping it internal
the trap of pattern-driven design where every construction problem becomes a factory

signals: team is debating which GoF creational pattern to use
code has mixed constructors, static factories, factories, builders, and globals without a clear rule
new product variants are expected but the axis of variation is vague
public API surface is growing around object creation

lenses.tension: It favors minimal mechanisms and may reject flexibility that another lens wants to reserve for future variants.

lenses.name: Behavior Layers
lenses.focus: Adds optional behavior by wrapping a stable interface instead of multiplying feature-specific subclasses or flags.
concerns: wrapper order and composition rules
transparent interface preservation
state ownership across layers
debuggability of call chains
configuration of optional capabilities
tests for each layer and common stacks

signals: features are controlled by many boolean flags
subclasses differ only by added responsibilities
behavior needs to be combined in different orders per deployment or consumer
core behavior is stable but optional capabilities keep changing
callers should depend on one interface regardless of enabled extras

lenses.tension: Keeps extension local but can create opaque wrapper stacks; the trap is making order-dependent decorators whose behavior cannot be reasoned about independently.

lenses.name: Policy Stack
lenses.focus: Uses decorators for cross-cutting policies such as validation, retries, caching, metrics, authorization, and tracing around a narrow capability.
concerns: policy ordering
idempotency and retry safety
cache key and invalidation boundaries
observability propagation
authorization placement
failure wrapping and exception transparency

signals: the same cross-cutting behavior is copied around many call sites
retry, cache, metric, or auth logic is tangled with business logic
teams need policy changes without modifying the core implementation
a narrow interface is called from many workflows
operational behavior varies by environment or tenant

lenses.tension: Centralizes policies but can obscure where behavior really occurs; the trap is layering retries, caching, and authorization without an explicit order contract.

lenses.name: Facade Audit
lenses.focus: Challenges whether a facade is simplifying real consumer tasks or merely hiding unresolved subsystem complexity behind a larger object.
concerns: facade method count and cohesion
leaked internal types
ambiguous convenience methods
hidden global state
testing through versus around the facade
subsystem ownership boundaries

signals: a facade keeps gaining unrelated methods
consumers still need internal subsystem objects after using the facade
the facade has become a transaction script dumping ground
tests mock the facade instead of verifying meaningful contracts
internal complexity is growing because consumers cannot see important distinctions

lenses.tension: May push complexity back into explicit concepts; the trap it guards against is mistaking a smaller public API for a better design.

lenses.name: Use-Case Facade
lenses.focus: Shapes a small task-oriented entrypoint over a complex subsystem so consumers get stable workflows instead of internal choreography.
concerns: consumer-facing use cases
method granularity
transaction and consistency boundary
error vocabulary
subsystem dependency hiding
versioning and backward compatibility

signals: callers must know the order of several subsystem calls
many consumers duplicate orchestration logic
a subsystem has too many public classes for normal use
internal refactors are blocked by broad consumer coupling
onboarding requires explaining internals before simple tasks can be done

lenses.tension: Improves ergonomics but may flatten important choices; the trap is creating a god facade that becomes the only place any workflow can live.

lenses.name: Factory Method Extension Seam
lenses.focus: Use Factory Method only when a stable algorithm or framework needs subclasses or extensions to choose one product implementation behind a common interface.
concerns: whether the base workflow genuinely owns the algorithm while extensions own product choice
product interface completeness so callers do not downcast after creation
factory method naming, visibility, and override granularity
constructor dependencies that leak through subclasses or force protected state
whether a registry or injected factory would be cleaner than inheritance
the trap of coupling creation policy to a subclass hierarchy that later needs independent variation

signals: base class or framework algorithm contains conditional construction of one product type
third parties need to provide product implementations without rewriting the algorithm
new variants arrive independently but share one narrow product contract
subclasses already exist for behavioral variation and creation is the missing hook

lenses.tension: It creates a crisp extension hook but pays with inheritance coupling and weaker composition when product choice should be runtime-configurable.

lenses.name: Intrinsic State
lenses.focus: Splits shared immutable intrinsic state from contextual extrinsic state to reduce memory, duplication, and construction cost at scale.
concerns: intrinsic versus extrinsic state boundary
immutability guarantees
identity and equality semantics
factory or pool ownership
cache eviction policy
thread safety and sharing scope

signals: the system creates many similar small objects
memory or allocation pressure is a known bottleneck
large repeated metadata is stored per instance
rendering, parsing, rules, symbols, permissions, or catalog items repeat heavily
object identity is less important than value plus context

lenses.tension: Trades simple object ownership for shared state discipline; the trap is sharing mutable state or optimizing before scale evidence exists.

lenses.name: Interpreter DSL Semantics
lenses.focus: Owns small domain languages or rule expressions by making grammar, evaluation, errors, and extension points explicit instead of embedding stringly conditionals.
concerns: grammar, AST, parser boundaries, and whether a DSL is justified over configuration or finite strategies
evaluation context, variable binding, determinism, sandboxing, and side-effect policy
type checking, validation, diagnostics, explainability, and source locations for user errors
operator precedence, compatibility, saved-expression versioning, and migration
performance through caching, compilation, partial evaluation, or bounded execution
trap avoidance for bespoke languages that outgrow their tooling, documentation, and security model

signals: users or administrators configure rules, filters, formulas, policies, or routing expressions
logic is stored as strings or scattered condition builders
expressions must be validated, explained, versioned, and safely evaluated before execution
a small rule language must evolve without breaking saved expressions

lenses.tension: Trades hard-coded logic for expressive user-configurable semantics; the trap is building a bespoke language when named strategies, a rule table, or ordinary configuration would be simpler.

lenses.name: Iterator Traversal Contract
lenses.focus: Owns traversal boundaries so clients consume collections, streams, or graphs without depending on storage layout or eager materialization.
concerns: traversal order, consistency guarantees, and whether ordering is part of the contract
lazy versus eager evaluation, pagination, cursors, and streaming boundaries
resource lifetime, cancellation, backpressure, and cleanup for remote or I/O-backed iteration
mutation while iterating and snapshot versus live-view semantics
client code that peeks into internal collection representation
leaky iterators that hide expensive database, network, or graph traversal costs

signals: clients duplicate traversal logic or reach into internal collections
large datasets need streaming, pagination, cursors, or bounded memory use
collection representation is changing but client traversal should remain stable
tree or graph traversal order affects correctness, performance, or user-visible output

lenses.tension: Trades direct structure access for an abstraction over traversal; the trap is promising simple iteration while hiding expensive, stateful, or failure-prone work.

lenses.name: Lifetime Scope Ownership
lenses.focus: Audit creational choices by the lifetime they imply: per call, per request, per tenant, per thread, pooled, cached, cloned, or process-wide.
concerns: who owns disposal, close, rollback, cancellation, or release of created objects
scope mismatch between object lifetime and request, tenant, transaction, or thread lifetime
caching and pooling boundaries versus fresh factory creation
lazy initialization races and duplicated expensive resources
whether prototypes or singletons accidentally cross isolation boundaries
the trap of solving creation syntax while leaving lifetime semantics undefined

signals: created objects hold files, sockets, transactions, locks, caches, GPU handles, or external clients
memory leaks, double-close bugs, or stale cached instances appear after refactors
same code must run in single-tenant, multi-tenant, batch, and request-scoped environments
a singleton, pool, factory, or clone choice changes concurrency behavior

lenses.tension: It prioritizes runtime correctness and ownership clarity over the simpler API shape another creation pattern may prefer.

lenses.name: Mediator Collaboration Hub
lenses.focus: Owns tangled peer-to-peer collaborations by centralizing coordination rules without turning the hub into a god object.
concerns: interaction graph reduction: which participants stop knowing about which peers
coordination responsibilities that belong in the mediator versus domain objects or services
explicit workflow state, ordering, concurrency, and failure handling across participants
participant contracts that stay narrow and do not expose internals to the mediator
testability of collaboration rules without constructing the whole application
god-object pressure when the mediator becomes the dumping ground for business logic

signals: components call each other in cycles or know too much about peer internals
UI widgets, services, or modules need coordinated updates across several participants
a business workflow spans peers but lacks a clear owner
adding one participant requires edits in many existing participants

lenses.tension: Trades peer coupling for central coordination; the trap is a mediator that silently becomes the largest and least cohesive object in the design.

lenses.name: Memento Snapshot
lenses.focus: Owns capturing restore points without exposing internals, especially for undo, rollback, draft editing, and recovery.
concerns: what state is captured, omitted, redacted, compressed, or recomputed
opaque snapshot format, versioning, migration, and compatibility with older saved states
memory, storage, retention, and cleanup policy for snapshot history
undo and redo integration with commands, side effects, and user-visible conflict handling
restore invariants, validation, transactional boundaries, and partial rollback semantics
security risk from snapshots retaining secrets, personal data, or stale authorization context

signals: users need undo, redo, draft recovery, or checkpoint restore
long-running workflows need safe rollback after failure
state must be restored without making internal fields part of the public contract
operations have reversible and irreversible parts that need explicit separation

lenses.tension: Trades operational recovery for storage, privacy, and invariant complexity; the trap is treating snapshots as free copies of mutable internals.

lenses.name: Constructor Sprawl Migration
lenses.focus: Refactor scattered constructors, static factories, and conditional new calls into a creational pattern without breaking callers or laundering old complexity into a new class.
concerns: call-site inventory and which construction shapes must remain source or binary compatible
deprecation path from constructors to builders, factories, or providers
adapter factories that preserve old behavior during transition
serialization, reflection, dependency injection, and framework construction hooks
tests that lock in old defaults before changing creation APIs
the trap of moving all conditionals into one god factory without reducing variability

signals: same object is constructed in many places with copied defaults or inconsistent setup
constructors or static factories are public and cannot be removed in one release
large refactor needs to introduce Builder, Factory Method, Abstract Factory, or Prototype incrementally
conditional creation logic is duplicated across application layers

lenses.tension: It optimizes for safe evolution and compatibility, which may preserve awkward transitional APIs longer than a clean-sheet pattern design would.

lenses.name: Observer Event Fanout
lenses.focus: Owns one-to-many notifications where the producer should publish facts without knowing every consumer.
concerns: event vocabulary, payload identity, correlation, ordering, and delivery guarantees
synchronous versus asynchronous dispatch and how listener failures affect the publisher
subscription lifecycle, unsubscription, memory leaks, and duplicate listener registration
idempotent consumers, retries, backpressure, and dead-letter handling when events cross process boundaries
observability for causality, including trace links from source action to listener side effects
prevention of hidden write-after-notify cycles that make state changes hard to reason about

signals: multiple consumers must react to the same domain or UI fact
the producer is accumulating optional callback dependencies
new side effects need to be attached without changing the source workflow
audit, metrics, projections, notifications, or cache invalidation follow primary actions

lenses.tension: Trades producer decoupling for harder causal tracing; the trap is unbounded side effects, vague delivery assumptions, and accidental distributed transactions.

lenses.name: Prototype Copy Contract
lenses.focus: Use Prototype when cloning a configured exemplar is the real abstraction, and make copy depth, identity, and shared state explicit.
concerns: deep copy versus shallow copy rules for every mutable field
which identities, handles, caches, timestamps, and subscriptions must be regenerated
whether shared references are immutable, intentionally pooled, or accidental aliases
clone customization points for small per-instance differences
performance comparison of clone versus fresh construction
the trap of hidden aliasing where copied objects accidentally share mutable state

signals: many instances share expensive setup and differ only in small local data
users or plugins define runtime templates that should be duplicated later
object graphs are constructed from exemplars, presets, scenes, documents, or workflows
factory code duplicates complex graph setup and then tweaks a few fields

lenses.tension: It can reduce construction cost and preserve runtime customization, but copy bugs are subtle and often worse than explicit construction.

lenses.name: Access Proxy
lenses.focus: Places a same-interface stand-in in front of sensitive objects to enforce authorization, validation, rate limits, or tenant boundaries.
concerns: permission checks before side effects
tenant and principal propagation
interface transparency
audit logging
bypass prevention
security tests against direct access

signals: callers need the same capability but with different permissions
sensitive operations are protected inconsistently across call sites
tenant or user context must be checked before object access
legacy code exposes direct references that should be mediated
auditing must be guaranteed for every operation

lenses.tension: Centralizes enforcement but can create false confidence if bypass paths remain; the trap is protecting the proxy while the real object is still reachable.

lenses.name: Failure Proxy
lenses.focus: Wraps remote, slow, or unreliable dependencies with a same-interface representative that owns latency, retries, circuit breaking, and fallback semantics.
concerns: timeout and cancellation policy
retry eligibility and idempotency
circuit breaker behavior
fallback data freshness
remote error translation
observability at the dependency boundary

signals: a dependency is remote, slow, expensive, or intermittently unavailable
callers duplicate timeout and retry logic
system reliability depends on isolating downstream failure
fallback or cached responses are acceptable for some operations
the real dependency should be replaceable in tests or local development

lenses.tension: Improves resilience but can change semantics through retries, caching, or fallbacks; the trap is pretending the proxy is transparent when failure behavior is part of the contract.

lenses.name: Lazy Proxy
lenses.focus: Defers expensive creation, loading, or remote fetching behind an object that preserves the expected interface while making materialization explicit enough to reason about.
concerns: trigger points for loading
cache lifetime
concurrency during first access
partial initialization failure
serialization behavior
visibility of hidden I/O costs

signals: objects are expensive to construct but often unused
large graphs are loaded eagerly
remote or disk I/O is hidden in constructors
startup time or memory pressure is dominated by rarely used dependencies
callers need a stable reference before the real object is available

lenses.tension: Reduces upfront cost but can move latency to surprising places; the trap is hiding I/O behind ordinary property access where callers cannot budget for it.

lenses.name: Singleton Lifecycle Containment
lenses.focus: Treat Singleton as a lifecycle and ownership claim, not a convenience accessor, and require proof that exactly one instance is valid for the process scope.
concerns: domain or runtime reason that one instance is required rather than merely convenient
initialization order, lazy loading, teardown, and restart behavior
thread safety, process forking, serialization, and multi-tenant assumptions
test reset hooks and isolation between test cases
whether dependency injection, a scoped provider, or an explicit context object would be safer
the trap of global state that hides dependencies and blocks parallelism

signals: design proposes getInstance, static global access, or a process-wide registry
resource truly has one process-wide owner such as a metrics sink, clock source, or hardware handle
tests are flaky because shared state persists across cases
concurrent initialization or shutdown races have appeared around a global object

lenses.tension: It gives simple access to a unique service but concentrates hidden coupling, lifecycle risk, and test pollution.

lenses.name: State Lifecycle Machine
lenses.focus: Owns behavior that changes by lifecycle phase and must make illegal transitions impossible or explicit.
concerns: finite states, transition events, guards, and terminal conditions instead of boolean flag soup
state-owned behavior for operations whose legality or meaning changes by phase
transition side effects, ordering, idempotency, and failure handling
persistence, migration, and versioning of state names and transition history
concurrency and race conditions around simultaneous transitions
avoiding class-per-state overkill when a simple enum and transition table is clearer

signals: flags combine into modes that are hard to reason about
bugs come from methods being allowed in the wrong status
lifecycle transitions drive permissions, side effects, SLAs, or external contracts
new states require careful migration, compatibility, or operational rollout

lenses.tension: Trades linear procedural code for an explicit transition model; the trap is hiding simple branching behind many tiny state classes or allowing transitions outside the model.

lenses.name: Strategy Policy Seam
lenses.focus: Owns places where a stable decision point must swap algorithms or policies without cloning the caller.
concerns: conditional ladders over mode, customer, tenant, feature flag, or provider with the same input-output shape
the smallest useful strategy interface and whether it carries only policy inputs instead of caller internals
selection mechanics through configuration, registry, factory, or dependency injection
shared invariants, validation, and error semantics that must not be duplicated across strategies
test matrices that prove variants are interchangeable where the caller expects interchangeability
guardrails against strategy proliferation when variation is speculative or cosmetic

signals: the same operation has multiple business policies selected at runtime
if/else or switch branches share setup but diverge in the algorithm
new variants arrive more often than callers change
tests duplicate the same scenario table across modes or providers

lenses.tension: Trades direct branching for an indirection point; the trap is inventing a hierarchy for speculative variants or leaking caller state into every strategy.

lenses.name: Template Method Skeleton
lenses.focus: Owns algorithms with fixed ordering and narrow extension hooks when the invariant sequence matters more than runtime composition.
concerns: the non-overridable skeleton steps that must always happen in order
hook methods with precise preconditions, postconditions, defaults, and failure behavior
resource handling, transactions, locks, cleanup, and telemetry that must wrap every variant
subclass coupling and whether inheritance is already part of the local design language
tests that prove subclasses cannot skip mandatory steps
fragile base-class risk from protected state, implicit call order, or broad hooks

signals: several implementations duplicate the same ordered workflow
customization points are stable, small, and known in advance
subclasses or implementations keep violating required setup, teardown, or validation steps
transaction, locking, authorization, or cleanup must wrap all variants consistently

lenses.tension: Trades hook simplicity for inheritance coupling; the trap is fragile base classes and hidden control flow when callers actually need runtime composition.

lenses.name: Testable Creation Seams
lenses.focus: Shape creational patterns so tests can substitute products, control side effects, and assert creation contracts without depending on globals or real resources.
concerns: explicit dependencies instead of hidden new calls inside business logic
factory, builder, and prototype contracts that can be tested with doubles
deterministic creation for clocks, random IDs, networking, files, and external clients
contract tests for product families and extension-provided implementations
avoiding service locator APIs that look like factories but hide global state

signals: object creation opens sockets, reads files, starts threads, allocates expensive resources, or calls external systems
unit tests require real collaborators because creation is buried inside logic
new variants need contract tests against a shared interface
singletons or static factories make tests order-dependent

lenses.tension: It increases substitutability and confidence but can introduce extra interfaces and over-mocking if creation is already simple.

lenses.name: Visitor Operation Matrix
lenses.focus: Owns adding cross-cutting operations over a stable object structure while keeping element types closed and operation logic centralized.
concerns: whether element types are stable enough to justify visitor friction
operations such as export, validation, rendering, analysis, or transformation that span every element type
double dispatch, traversal separation, result aggregation, and error propagation
exhaustive handling of variants and compile-time or test-time detection of missed cases
compatibility costs when adding a new element type across many visitors
avoidance of scattered type switches and casts that already act like informal visitors

signals: the same tree, AST, schema, or domain graph needs exporters, analyzers, validators, or renderers
operations are added more often than node or element types
code has scattered type switches over a mostly closed hierarchy
the team needs exhaustive handling of variants for correctness or compatibility

lenses.tension: Trades localized element behavior for centralized operations; the trap is severe churn whenever the element taxonomy is still evolving.

lenses.name: API Evolution Compatibility
lenses.focus: Owns how public and extension APIs can evolve while preserving source, binary, behavioral, and operational compatibility where promised.
concerns: Identifies compatibility dimensions relevant to the ecosystem
Checks default methods, overloads, adapters, and shims for additive evolution
Defines deprecation policy, removal criteria, and communication requirements
Prevents behavioral changes from hiding behind type-compatible signatures
Requires compatibility tests across representative old consumers
Documents migration recipes for breaking or high-friction changes

signals: the API has released consumers or published extension contracts
a refactor changes signatures, defaults, serialization, ordering, or failure semantics
maintainers need a major-version or migration plan
downstream users compile or deploy against multiple host versions

lenses.tension: Can preserve old mistakes longer than ideal and slow cleanup of weak abstractions.

lenses.name: Configuration Surface Budget
lenses.focus: Owns preventing extension flexibility from turning into an unbounded configuration matrix.
concerns: Separates required configuration from optional tuning and experimental knobs
Rejects flags that should be named strategies or capability choices
Checks default values for safety, portability, and future compatibility
Groups related settings around user intent instead of implementation modules
Defines validation, precedence, and override rules
Requires configuration examples for common extension scenarios

signals: new behavior is mostly added through flags, properties, or environment variables
consumers cannot predict which settings interact
support issues come from misconfiguration rather than missing features
extensions need tenant-specific or deployment-specific tuning

lenses.tension: Can resist legitimate configurability when deployments have real operational differences.

lenses.name: Consumer Escape Hatch Design
lenses.focus: Owns intentional escape hatches that unblock advanced consumers without making unsupported internals look like stable API.
concerns: Names escape hatches explicitly and documents their stability limits
Requires safe defaults before exposing lower-level override mechanisms
Constrains raw access through adapters, scopes, or capability grants
Ensures escape hatches do not bypass validation, authorization, or invariants
Provides migration paths from common escape-hatch usage into supported APIs
Tracks which advanced use cases justify future first-class extension points

signals: advanced consumers have legitimate needs beyond the curated API
teams are forking the library or monkey-patching internals
maintainers cannot predict all integrations yet
strict public APIs are blocking adoption for edge cases

lenses.tension: Creates a pressure valve that can become a de facto public contract if widely used.

lenses.name: Contract Test Harness
lenses.focus: Owns giving extension authors and host maintainers executable contracts that prove an implementation conforms to the extension API.
concerns: Defines reusable conformance tests for each strategy, plugin, or provider interface
Covers lifecycle, error, concurrency, cancellation, and boundary cases
Provides fixtures and fake host contexts that match public contracts
Checks compatibility of old extensions against new host versions
Separates contract tests from host implementation tests
Makes failure output actionable for extension authors

signals: third-party implementations must behave consistently
extension bugs are discovered only after integration
host maintainers need confidence before changing extension contracts
the API has subtle ordering, idempotency, streaming, or failure semantics

lenses.tension: Raises the cost of adding or changing extension points because contracts become executable obligations.

lenses.name: Extender Error Model
lenses.focus: Extension APIs need a stable way to distinguish host faults, plugin faults, validation failures, retries, and user-facing errors without leaking internals.

lenses.name: Extension Point Minimality
lenses.focus: Owns keeping extension points narrow, intentional, and stable enough to support open-closed evolution without exposing the whole implementation.
concerns: Distinguishes true extension points from accidental public hooks
Limits what extension code can observe, mutate, or override
Requires each extension point to have a named use case and compatibility promise
Prefers capability-specific interfaces over broad service objects
Checks that new behavior can be added without editing stable host code
Removes speculative hooks that lack concrete consumers

signals: public APIs are growing faster than actual consumer needs
internal objects are being passed to callbacks or plugins
extension requests are vague or speculative
maintainers are worried about backwards compatibility obligations

lenses.tension: May slow down experimental consumers who want broad access to internals.

lenses.name: Extension Sandbox Boundary
lenses.focus: Owns limiting what extension code can damage through isolation, permissions, resource limits, and controlled host access.
concerns: Defines what extensions may read, mutate, call, allocate, and persist
Checks isolation boundaries for untrusted, semi-trusted, and first-party extensions
Specifies timeout, cancellation, concurrency, and backpressure behavior
Prevents extension callbacks from reentering unsafe host state
Designs permission grants and audit trails for sensitive operations
Ensures host invariants survive extension exceptions and partial execution

signals: extensions come from third parties or less trusted teams
extension code can access customer data, credentials, network, filesystem, or billing paths
a plugin failure could compromise availability or correctness
callbacks run inside transactions, locks, or critical host workflows

lenses.tension: Can reduce plugin power and increase implementation complexity for trusted in-process extensions.

lenses.name: Fluent Consumer Happy Path
lenses.focus: Owns the default consumer experience so the common case is obvious, short, discoverable, and hard to misuse.
concerns: Designs the smallest working setup path for a new consumer
Checks naming, method order, defaults, overloads, and autocomplete flow
Ensures advanced extension hooks do not pollute the common path
Validates error messages and required configuration from the caller's perspective
Prefers progressive disclosure over mandatory expert-level setup
Requires examples that compile and reflect production usage

signals: the API will be adopted by teams outside the authors
setup requires many concepts before a basic example works
configuration objects expose rare knobs next to required fields
existing consumers copy large boilerplate blocks

lenses.tension: Can hide important extension mechanics behind convenience layers that later constrain advanced use.

lenses.name: Generic Type Boundary
lenses.focus: Owns whether generic parameters, associated types, or type constraints make extension code safer without making consumers decode the type system.
concerns: Validates that generic parameters model real domain variability
Prevents leaking implementation types through public signatures
Checks variance, constraints, defaults, and inference at consumer call sites
Avoids generic parameter explosion across layered APIs
Ensures type-safe extension points still support mocking and testing
Documents when dynamic dispatch is preferable to generic specialization

signals: API consumers must supply domain-specific model, transport, command, or result types
current APIs rely on casts, untyped maps, reflection, or stringly typed payloads
generic signatures are becoming difficult to call or document
extension authors need compile-time guarantees across multiple related types

lenses.tension: Can trade runtime flexibility and approachability for stronger static guarantees.

lenses.name: Minimal Public API
lenses.focus: Owns drawing a hard boundary between supported API, semi-private integration surface, and implementation detail.
concerns: Classifies exported types, methods, events, and configuration keys by stability promise
Removes accidental public exposure caused by package layout or visibility defaults
Prevents public APIs from returning mutable internals
Requires naming and documentation to signal support level
Checks that tests exercise public contracts instead of implementation details
Ensures extension seams do not bypass validation or invariants

signals: library consumers are depending on internals
minor releases regularly break downstream users
packages export more surface than maintainers intend to support
extension hooks expose host state directly

lenses.tension: Can frustrate power users who need capabilities that have not yet been formalized.

lenses.name: Extension Observability
lenses.focus: Plugin and extension ecosystems need per-extension logs, metrics, traces, attribution, and failure isolation; several existing lenses reference this missing perspective.

lenses.name: Plugin Contract Lifecycle
lenses.focus: Owns the end-to-end lifecycle of plugins from discovery through activation, compatibility checks, execution, failure, and shutdown.
concerns: Defines stable plugin metadata, capability declaration, and compatibility negotiation
Checks discovery and registration paths for deterministic ordering and diagnosability
Separates plugin loading from plugin execution and host orchestration
Specifies failure isolation, disabled-state behavior, and partial availability
Ensures plugin teardown and resource cleanup are explicit
Avoids letting plugins depend on unstable host internals

signals: third parties or separate teams will add behavior after release
extensions need runtime discovery or optional installation
host code must survive plugin failure or version skew
plugins may depend on external resources, credentials, or lifecycle hooks

lenses.tension: Adds ceremony and operational surface compared with compile-time extension seams.

lenses.name: Runtime Capability Negotiation
lenses.focus: Owns how optional behaviors are discovered and selected at runtime without hard dependencies or brittle type checks.
concerns: Defines capability descriptors, predicates, and priority rules
Avoids instanceof chains, reflection probes, and name-based feature checks
Handles missing, partial, duplicate, and conflicting capabilities deterministically
Separates capability discovery from authorization and configuration
Provides diagnostics explaining why an extension was or was not selected
Ensures fallback behavior is explicit and tested

signals: available integrations vary by deployment, tenant, provider, or installed module
the host must choose among multiple extension implementations
optional features cannot be represented cleanly at compile time
current code probes for methods, class names, flags, or environment details

lenses.tension: Shifts safety from compile-time types to runtime validation and diagnostics.

lenses.name: Strategy Seam Inventory
lenses.focus: Owns the decision of which behaviors must vary through explicit strategies instead of inheritance, flags, or scattered conditionals.
concerns: Identifies variation points that deserve first-class strategy interfaces
Rejects boolean flags and mode enums that encode future extension needs
Checks strategy construction, dependency injection, and lifecycle ownership
Ensures default strategies are usable without custom wiring
Separates stable orchestration flow from replaceable policy decisions
Verifies strategies can be tested in isolation with contract fixtures

signals: multiple customers need different behavior for the same workflow
conditionals are accumulating around provider, tenant, format, policy, or environment
subclassing is the current extension mechanism
new variants require editing central orchestration code

lenses.tension: Can over-fragment simple logic into too many tiny abstractions before the variation is real.

lenses.name: Versioned Extension Protocol
lenses.focus: Owns compatibility rules for extension points so hosts and extensions can evolve independently across releases.
concerns: Defines semantic versioning or capability-version rules for extension contracts
Separates additive evolution from breaking changes and deprecations
Specifies compatibility checks before extension activation
Requires migration paths for deprecated hooks and data shapes
Designs feature detection instead of brittle version comparisons where possible
Documents support windows and failure behavior for incompatible extensions

signals: extensions may be compiled, deployed, or upgraded separately from the host
public hooks already have consumers that cannot all move in lockstep
backwards compatibility is a release requirement
extension payloads or callbacks are expected to change over time

lenses.tension: Introduces compatibility machinery that can feel heavy before multiple released versions exist.

lenses.name: Adapter Anti-Corruption
lenses.focus: Owns adapter translations so external protocols, schemas, errors, and lifecycle quirks cannot leak into the domain model.
concerns: Mapping between external DTOs, persistence rows, messages, SDK objects, and domain concepts
Translation of external errors, retries, timeouts, nulls, pagination, and partial responses
Keeping adapter-specific annotations, generated types, and client models outside the core
Anti-corruption layers for legacy systems and third-party APIs with alien semantics
Adapter ownership of observability, credentials, rate limits, and protocol mechanics
Round-trip mapping tests for lossy conversions and versioned external contracts

signals: domain code contains vendor names, HTTP status codes, SQL rows, or queue metadata
external schema changes force domain refactors
legacy terminology is spreading into new bounded contexts
integration bugs come from implicit mapping assumptions

lenses.tension: Reduces semantic coupling to external systems but adds translation code and can obscure useful protocol capabilities.

lenses.name: Aggregate Boundary Invariants
lenses.focus: Owns aggregate cuts so every strong invariant has exactly one transactional home and no aggregate becomes a convenience object graph.
concerns: Whether aggregate roots align with real consistency boundaries rather than database tables or screens
Which invariants must be enforced synchronously inside one aggregate transaction
Rules for referencing other aggregates by identity rather than object navigation
Lifecycle operations that should be methods on the root instead of scattered setters
Aggregate size, write contention, lock scope, and accidental all-in-one roots
Factory and reconstitution paths that cannot create invalid aggregate states

signals: business rules span multiple entities and failures leave partial state
models are anemic records with service-layer invariant checks
transactions routinely update large object graphs
teams disagree about where a command is allowed to mutate state

lenses.tension: Favors strong local consistency and explicit mutation rules, which can reduce query convenience and push cross-aggregate workflows toward eventual consistency.

lenses.name: Use Case Orchestration
lenses.focus: Owns the application layer as a thin command and query orchestration boundary around domain behavior, transactions, authorization, and ports.
concerns: Command and query shapes that express user intent without leaking UI or transport details
Transaction demarcation around one aggregate change or an explicit process boundary
Placement of authorization, idempotency, retries, clocks, identity, and unit-of-work concerns
Preventing application services from becoming procedural domain models
Read-model shortcuts that do not contaminate write-side invariants
Consistent result and error semantics for callers

signals: controllers, jobs, or message handlers contain business orchestration
domain objects call infrastructure services directly
transaction boundaries are implicit or inconsistent across entry points
the same use case is duplicated for REST, CLI, batch, and events

lenses.tension: Centralizes orchestration and policies but can become a pass-through layer if commands are too CRUD-shaped or the domain has little behavior.

lenses.name: Executable Seam Fences
lenses.focus: Owns automated architecture tests that make dependency direction, package ownership, and forbidden imports fail fast in CI.
concerns: Rules that domain packages cannot import adapters, frameworks, generated clients, or persistence details
Rules that adapters cannot be used directly by other adapters except through ports or composition code
Allowed dependency matrices between contexts, layers, modules, and test fixtures
Naming and package conventions that make violations easy to detect and hard to rationalize
CI integration, baselines, exception lists, and ratcheting for legacy code
Architecture tests that check behaviorally meaningful seams instead of brittle folder trivia

signals: architecture diagrams drift from code within weeks
reviews repeatedly catch the same layer or dependency violations
a refactor depends on keeping new boundaries from regressing
the codebase is large enough that manual seam policing does not scale

lenses.tension: Turns architecture into executable constraints but can become noisy if rules encode aspirational design before the code is ready.

lenses.name: Bounded Context Language
lenses.focus: Owns context boundaries so each model has a clear language, ownership, integration contract, and reason to change.
concerns: Context maps across upstream, downstream, conformist, anti-corruption, and shared-kernel relationships
Words that mean different things in sales, billing, fulfillment, identity, or operations
Package, module, schema, and team boundaries that match business capabilities
Where duplicated concepts are acceptable versus where a shared kernel is justified
Integration events and published language between contexts
Migration paths for splitting a muddled model without big-bang rewrites

signals: one model serves multiple subdomains with conflicting rules
teams argue over the meaning of common nouns like account, order, customer, or status
changes for one capability repeatedly break another capability
services share database tables or internal classes across ownership boundaries

lenses.tension: Clarifies ownership and autonomy but may duplicate concepts and require explicit integration work where a single shared model was convenient.

lenses.name: Context Modularization Path
lenses.focus: Owns the incremental path from tangled packages or a modular monolith toward enforceable bounded-context modules without needless distribution.
concerns: Module boundaries that can be enforced in-process before service extraction is considered
Public APIs between modules and private internals that callers cannot import
Dependency cycles, shared utility packages, and cross-context transaction assumptions
Strangler steps that move one use case, aggregate, or adapter at a time
Build, packaging, and ownership changes that make boundaries visible
Decision criteria for staying modular monolith versus extracting a deployable service

signals: a monolith has unclear package boundaries but service extraction feels premature
contexts have been identified but code still depends across internals
dependency cycles block tests, builds, or independent releases
teams need a migration sequence rather than a target architecture diagram

lenses.tension: Optimizes for incremental enforceability and deployability choices, but may defer deeper domain modeling work until seams are stable.

lenses.name: Domain Events and Consistency
lenses.focus: Owns the line between in-aggregate decisions, cross-aggregate processes, domain events, integration events, and eventual consistency mechanisms.
concerns: Events named as business facts rather than technical notifications
Which side effects must happen in the same transaction and which can be asynchronous
Outbox, inbox, deduplication, ordering, replay, and idempotent handlers
Process managers or sagas for long-running workflows that cross aggregate or context boundaries
Mapping domain events to public integration events without leaking internal state
Failure semantics when downstream systems reject, lag, or duplicate messages

signals: commands update multiple aggregates to keep workflow state in sync
event handlers are hard to replay or safely retry
teams confuse internal domain events with public message contracts
invariants are being enforced through eventually consistent subscribers

lenses.tension: Improves decoupling across boundaries but introduces delivery semantics, temporal coupling, and harder debugging.

lenses.name: Hexagonal Contract Tests
lenses.focus: Owns the test pyramid for hexagonal systems: pure domain tests, use-case tests with fake ports, adapter contract tests, and architecture seam tests.
concerns: Fast unit tests for aggregate invariants and value object laws
Use-case tests that substitute outbound ports without mocking domain internals
Provider and consumer contracts for adapters, APIs, messages, and persistence mappings
Golden tests for anti-corruption translations and error normalization
Architecture tests for dependency rules and context boundaries
Test fixture builders that preserve invariants instead of bypassing constructors

signals: most tests are slow end-to-end scenarios because seams are unclear
mocks mirror implementation details and break during harmless refactors
adapter changes frequently break callers at runtime
architecture rules exist only in review comments or documentation

lenses.tension: Raises confidence in boundaries and contracts but can slow change when contract ownership and fixture maintenance are unclear.

lenses.name: Persistence Boundary Pragmatism
lenses.focus: Owns the practical boundary between rich domain models and storage realities so repositories reconstitute aggregates without letting the database own the design.
concerns: Repository contracts scoped to aggregate roots rather than arbitrary table access
Separating write-model persistence from read-model projections and reporting queries
Avoiding lazy-loading, active record, ORM annotations, and transaction proxies in domain behavior
Mapping aggregate identity, versioning, optimistic concurrency, snapshots, and deletes
Choosing when direct data access is acceptable for read paths or low-risk CRUD
Migration strategies for schemas that do not yet match aggregate boundaries

signals: ORM entities are treated as the domain model and leak across layers
query needs are distorting aggregate design
