repository interfaces expose table-shaped CRUD for every entity
storage migrations are blocking a cleaner domain cut

lenses.tension: Balances domain purity against delivery cost, which can intentionally permit read-side shortcuts that stricter hexagonal lenses would reject.

lenses.name: Policy Placement Discipline
lenses.focus: Owns where business policy lives, distinguishing entity behavior, aggregate methods, domain services, specifications, application services, and adapter policies.
concerns: Moving invariant logic out of controllers, adapters, repositories, and transaction scripts
Choosing domain services only for policies that truly span multiple objects or external facts
Keeping specifications and rules composable without creating a generic rule-engine trap
Separating business decisions from technical policies like retries, caching, and authorization plumbing
Avoiding both anemic domain models and overstuffed aggregate roots
Naming policies in business language so tests read as examples

signals: the same business rule appears in multiple services or adapters
review debates center on whether logic belongs in the model or service layer
aggregate roots are either empty data bags or contain unrelated workflow logic
new rules require edits across persistence, transport, and domain code

lenses.tension: Improves rule locality and model expressiveness but can over-model simple procedural workflows if applied indiscriminately.

lenses.name: Port Direction Discipline
lenses.focus: Owns dependency inversion so the domain and application core define stable inbound and outbound ports while adapters depend inward.
concerns: Whether inbound ports represent use cases instead of protocol-specific controllers
Whether outbound ports are owned by the core and named in domain language
No imports from frameworks, persistence, messaging, or HTTP packages into the core
Port granularity that avoids both chatty interfaces and god gateways
Synchronous versus asynchronous port semantics and failure contracts
Composition root placement for dependency wiring without hidden service locators

signals: business logic imports ORM, web, queue, cloud, or filesystem APIs
tests require full infrastructure to exercise core behavior
adding a new delivery mechanism duplicates use-case logic
dependency graphs point outward from domain to adapters

lenses.tension: Protects the core from infrastructure churn but may introduce interface ceremony when there is only one stable adapter and low volatility.

lenses.name: Value Object Language
lenses.focus: Owns the replacement of ambiguous primitives with immutable domain concepts that encode validation, equality, and ubiquitous language.
concerns: Primitive obsession in identifiers, money, quantities, dates, ranges, status reasons, and external codes
Construction rules that make invalid values unrepresentable inside the domain
Value equality, normalization, formatting, and serialization boundaries
Whether names reflect business language instead of transport or database terminology
Avoiding behaviorless wrapper proliferation when a primitive is already sufficient
Keeping parsing and locale or protocol concerns outside value objects

signals: the same string or number means different things in different paths
validation is duplicated across controllers, services, and adapters
bugs come from units, currency, time zones, ranges, or identifier mixups
domain APIs expose maps, tuples, or loose DTOs instead of named concepts

lenses.tension: Improves semantic precision but can add type volume, mapping code, and friction at serialization boundaries.

lenses.name: Abstraction Economy
lenses.focus: Remove speculative, leaky, or one-use abstractions until the code says only what the system truly needs.
concerns: interfaces with one implementation and no credible variation
generic types that obscure simple data flow
inheritance hierarchies used for code reuse instead of substitutability
configuration-driven behavior that hides straightforward decisions
helper layers that merely pass through arguments
abstractions named after patterns instead of domain responsibilities

signals: readers must jump through many files to understand simple behavior
a design was built for hypothetical reuse that never arrived
types are generic but all call sites use the same concrete shape
changes require modifying both abstraction and implementation every time
tests mostly assert adapter pass-through behavior

lenses.tension: Can remove seams that would have helped testing, substitution, or upcoming product variation.

lenses.name: Callsite Readability
lenses.focus: Judge APIs and helpers by whether ordinary call sites read correctly without opening the implementation.
concerns: boolean and positional parameters that hide meaning
methods whose names do not reveal side effects, ordering, or failure behavior
parameter lists that mix required data, options, and dependencies
return values that require caller-side interpretation of magic values
fluent chains or builders that obscure required fields
call sites that repeat comments to explain what arguments mean

signals: callers pass literals whose meaning is unclear
the same API is misused in different parts of the codebase
new contributors need to inspect implementations for ordinary usage
public methods have grown long parameter lists
reviewers request wrapper helpers just to make calls understandable

lenses.tension: Can make internals slightly more verbose by introducing named option objects or domain-specific value types.

lenses.name: Change Locality Map
lenses.focus: Optimize the design so expected future changes land in a small, predictable set of places.
concerns: shotgun surgery across handlers, mappers, tests, and configuration
parallel inheritance or parallel enum handling spread across files
feature switches that leak into unrelated modules
copy-pasted workflows that diverge over time
missing extension points for known variation
public APIs that force consumers to duplicate internal decision logic

signals: adding one case requires edits in many files
previous similar features caused regressions in unrelated areas
new product variants or integrations are expected
the same condition appears in multiple modules
code ownership is unclear because changes cross boundaries

lenses.tension: Can overfit to predicted future changes and introduce indirection before variation is real enough to justify it.

lenses.name: Cohesive Change Ownership
lenses.focus: Ensure each module, class, and function has one clear reason to change and owns a coherent slice of behavior.
concerns: god objects that mix orchestration, policy, persistence, validation, and formatting
classes grouped by technical convenience instead of change reason
methods that operate mostly on another object's data
state and behavior separated so invariants are enforced nowhere
files that attract unrelated edits across many features
responsibilities named too broadly to guide future placement

signals: a single file or class keeps growing with every feature
developers are unsure where new behavior belongs
bug fixes routinely touch unrelated responsibilities
domain rules are scattered across handlers, utilities, and services
review comments often say this feels like the wrong place

lenses.tension: Can increase the number of types and files, and may slow very small changes if boundaries become too fine-grained.

lenses.name: Comments To Contracts
lenses.focus: Treat comments that describe required behavior as missing types, assertions, tests, validations, or API contracts.
concerns: comments explaining invariants the code does not enforce
preconditions and postconditions hidden in prose
stale comments that contradict implementation
TODOs that mask correctness or lifecycle obligations
comments compensating for unclear names or tangled control flow
tribal operational constraints not captured by tests or configuration

signals: important behavior is documented only in comments
comments say must, never, always, only, or assume
bug fixes update prose without adding enforcement
callers must remember undocumented ordering or lifecycle rules
tests do not cover constraints described near the code

lenses.tension: May add validation or test scaffolding where a short explanatory comment would be sufficient for genuinely non-executable context.

lenses.name: Coupling Direction Control
lenses.focus: Force dependencies to point in deliberate directions through narrow contracts instead of incidental concrete knowledge.
concerns: bidirectional imports or reference cycles
high-level policy depending on low-level framework details
wide interfaces that expose implementation vocabulary
global state, service locators, or ambient context hiding real dependencies
tests requiring large object graphs because seams are misplaced
utility modules that become unowned shared dependency hubs

signals: a small change requires understanding many downstream modules
mocking or testing requires constructing unrelated dependencies
imports form cycles or architectural layers are blurred
business logic knows about transport, storage, or UI details
reusing a component outside its original context is painful

lenses.tension: Can introduce interfaces or adapters that feel indirect when the dependency is genuinely stable and local.

lenses.name: Cyclomatic Collapse
lenses.focus: Reduce branching complexity until each path is visible, named, and testable without mental simulation.
concerns: deep nesting and mixed happy-path/error-path control flow
conditionals that combine policy, null handling, permissions, and state transitions
duplicated branches with small variations
boolean flag parameters that multiply execution paths
switch statements that should be data-driven or polymorphic
uncovered edge paths hidden inside long methods

signals: a method is hard to test because path count explodes
new cases are added by copying and modifying branches
reviewers trace code manually to prove behavior
bugs appear only for specific combinations of flags or states
static analysis reports high cyclomatic or cognitive complexity

lenses.tension: Can split logic into too many tiny helpers if path extraction is done mechanically instead of around named decisions.

lenses.name: Duplication Decision Audit
lenses.focus: Distinguish harmful duplicated knowledge from acceptable repeated shape, then consolidate only the knowledge that must change together.
concerns: copied business rules that will drift
duplicated validation, mapping, authorization, or state-transition logic
premature DRY abstractions over code that only looks similar
constants or literals repeated without ownership
test duplication that hides fixture intent
shared helpers that couple unrelated workflows

signals: two bug fixes had to be applied in multiple places
similar code blocks have started to diverge accidentally
reviewers disagree whether duplication or abstraction is worse
shared utilities are difficult to name clearly
rule changes require search-and-edit across the repository

lenses.tension: Can either under-abstract and leave drift risk, or over-abstract and couple workflows that should evolve separately.

lenses.name: Naming Intent Audit
lenses.focus: Make names carry intent, domain meaning, side effects, and units so readers need less surrounding context.
concerns: ambiguous verbs such as handle, process, manage, update, and sync
names that describe implementation mechanics instead of purpose
boolean, status, and mode names that obscure polarity or lifecycle
missing units, time zones, currencies, or normalization state in names
inconsistent domain vocabulary across layers
abbreviations and overloaded terms that force tribal knowledge

signals: reviewers ask what a variable or method actually means
multiple names exist for the same domain concept
boolean flags or enum states are frequently misread
comments merely restate unclear names
bugs arise from unit, lifecycle, or side-effect confusion

lenses.tension: Can create noisy churn in public APIs or large diffs if renames are not scoped to meaningful confusion.

lenses.name: State Model Clarity
lenses.focus: Make lifecycle, validity, and transition states explicit so code does not rely on scattered flags and implicit ordering.
concerns: clusters of booleans that encode hidden states
invalid combinations representable in ordinary data structures
state transitions implemented as ad hoc field mutation
temporal coupling where methods must be called in a specific order
nullable fields whose meaning changes by phase
error and recovery states mixed with normal workflow state

signals: bugs come from impossible or contradictory state combinations
callers must check several flags before invoking behavior
comments describe object lifecycle phases
new statuses or workflow steps are being added
tests require elaborate setup sequences before assertions

lenses.tension: Can require broader model changes than a localized conditional fix, especially when persisted data or public APIs expose the current shape.

lenses.name: Straight-Line Implementation
lenses.focus: Prefer direct, locally readable code paths where the behavior is stable, simple, and not a true extension point.
concerns: unnecessary callbacks, hooks, registries, or strategy dispatch
control flow split across files without meaningful variation
framework-like designs inside application code
asynchronous or evented paths used where synchronous flow is clearer
helpers that hide the main algorithm rather than clarify it
configuration replacing code for decisions that are not operationally variable

signals: the design is harder to read than the behavior it implements
there is no real second implementation for extension seams
debugging requires following callbacks or registrations
new contributors cannot find the main path
abstraction exists mainly to satisfy a pattern preference

lenses.tension: Can reduce flexibility if applied to code that genuinely needs runtime substitution, plugin behavior, or policy separation.

lenses.name: Async Workflow Operability
lenses.focus: Expose progress, lag, retries, ownership, and replay safety for queues, schedulers, streams, batches, and long-running workflows.
concerns: Stable job, batch, message, partition, checkpoint, and causation identifiers
Metrics for queue depth, age, lag, throughput, retry count, dead-letter volume, and processing latency
Progress reports for long-running operations with completed, skipped, failed, and pending units
Retry, poison-message, dead-letter, replay, and compensation semantics visible to operators
Idempotency and deduplication observability for at-least-once delivery and backfills
Safe pause, resume, drain, backpressure, and priority controls

signals: background jobs fail silently or only surface through customer-visible delay
queues build up without clear owner, cause, or recovery path
batch jobs need restart, replay, or partial completion handling
stream processors use checkpoints, watermarks, or dead-letter queues
operators need to pause or drain work during incidents and migrations

lenses.tension: Adds strong operational control for non-request work, but may be overkill for simple synchronous paths or short-lived tasks.

lenses.name: Error Taxonomy Recovery
lenses.focus: Classify failures by semantics and recovery path so code, APIs, logs, metrics, and alerts agree on what an error means.
concerns: Stable categories for validation, authorization, conflict, not found, dependency, timeout, rate limit, invariant, and bug failures
Retryable versus permanent semantics tied to idempotency and side-effect boundaries
Mapping from internal errors to API status codes, result reports, log levels, and metrics labels
Preservation of cause chains without leaking implementation details or sensitive data
Explicit handling for cancellation, deadline exceeded, overload, and client-aborted work
Test coverage for taxonomy mapping and recovery behavior on important failure paths

signals: all failures are treated as generic exceptions or generic 500 responses
retry logic guesses from message strings or transport codes
alerts fire for expected user or validation errors
operators cannot tell dependency outages from product bugs
public APIs expose inconsistent error semantics across endpoints

lenses.tension: Adds semantic clarity and safer recovery, but taxonomy debates can become over-modeled if the system has few meaningful recovery paths.

lenses.name: Health Readiness Semantics
lenses.focus: Specify health, readiness, startup, and drain checks as operational contracts rather than generic ping endpoints.
concerns: Clear distinction between liveness, readiness, startup, dependency health, and degraded service state
Probe behavior during migrations, warmup, cache priming, leader election, and graceful shutdown
Dependency check depth that avoids cascading outages from overly strict readiness gates
Machine-readable status payloads with component names, severities, and remediation hints
Load balancer, orchestrator, and deployment semantics for fail-open versus fail-closed behavior
Manual or automated drain controls for maintenance, backpressure, and overload protection

signals: deployments route traffic before the service is actually ready
health checks pass while critical dependencies or background workers are broken
one dependency outage causes healthy instances to be removed from service
rolling deploys, schema migrations, or cache warmups cause transient customer impact
operators lack a safe way to drain or degrade a node

lenses.tension: Adds explicit operational states and deployment contracts, but overly clever checks can create fragility or false negatives.

lenses.name: Incident Debuggability
lenses.focus: Design the system so an on-call engineer can reconstruct state, decisions, and recent changes during an incident without code archaeology.
concerns: Breadcrumbs for state transitions, external calls, retries, lock acquisition, feature flags, and important decisions
Diagnostic snapshots or support bundles that capture safe effective configuration and runtime state
Runbook links, owner metadata, and remediation hints in alerts, reports, and administrative surfaces
Correlation between deploys, migrations, configuration changes, and observed symptoms
Debug endpoints or commands gated by authorization and safe output limits
Failure reproduction aids such as request identifiers, replay-safe inputs, and deterministic decision traces

signals: incidents require paging original authors to interpret behavior
recent deploys or configuration changes are hard to correlate with failures
support cannot gather enough context without direct database or shell access
complex state machines or decision engines fail without explaining their choices
systems run in customer, edge, or restricted environments with limited live access

lenses.tension: Optimizes for incident speed and supportability, but can add privileged surfaces and verbose breadcrumbs that require governance.

lenses.name: Metric SLI Modeling
lenses.focus: Define low-cardinality metrics around user-visible behavior, resource pressure, and dependency health before adding dashboards.
concerns: RED or equivalent request metrics for rate, errors, latency, and saturation on user-facing paths
USE or equivalent infrastructure metrics for utilization, saturation, and errors on constrained resources
Explicit SLI numerators, denominators, units, windows, and aggregation semantics
Separation between product analytics, business counters, and operational health metrics
Label cardinality budgets for tenants, users, resources, error codes, and routes
Instrumentation placement that avoids double counting retries, fan-out calls, and cached responses

signals: dashboards show many graphs but no clear service health answer
teams cannot quantify whether a change improved reliability or latency
alerts are based on CPU or raw exception count instead of user impact
high-cardinality metrics threaten cost or backend stability
batch, queue, or API behavior lacks throughput and latency baselines

lenses.tension: Favors compact aggregate signal over rich per-event detail, so it can under-serve deep debugging unless paired with logs or traces.

lenses.name: Runtime State Inspection
lenses.focus: Provide safe ways to inspect effective runtime configuration, dependency versions, feature flags, and operational mode.
concerns: Build, version, commit, schema, dependency, and feature-flag metadata attached to telemetry and status surfaces
Effective configuration inspection with source, override, default, and redaction semantics
Runtime mode visibility for read-only, degraded, maintenance, draining, leader, follower, and backfill states
Change audit for configuration, flags, rollout cohorts, and operational controls
Access-controlled admin surfaces with bounded output and no secret disclosure
Comparison support between instances, environments, or time windows during rollout debugging

signals: different instances behave differently and nobody can see effective configuration
feature flags or rollout cohorts complicate incident diagnosis
deploy, schema, or dependency version drift is a recurring source of failures
operators need to confirm degraded, maintenance, or read-only mode remotely
support environments have limited shell access

lenses.tension: Greatly improves explainability of live behavior, but expands administrative surface area and can leak sensitive configuration if not constrained.

lenses.name: SLO Alert Design
lenses.focus: Make alerts actionable, symptom-oriented, owner-routed, and tied to explicit user-impact budgets.
concerns: SLOs and error budgets for availability, latency, correctness, freshness, and durability where relevant
Symptom-based alert rules before cause-based component alerts
Burn-rate, sustained-threshold, or anomaly policies that match urgency and noise tolerance
Routing metadata for service owner, severity, business impact, and escalation path
Alert descriptions with current state, likely causes, dashboards, runbooks, and rollback hints
Suppression, deduplication, and maintenance-window behavior for deploys and known incidents

signals: on-call receives noisy alerts that do not require action
customer-impacting incidents are discovered by users before monitoring
alert severity is inconsistent across teams or services
dashboards exist but there is no paging policy
leadership needs reliability targets tied to product behavior

lenses.tension: Prioritizes operational focus and low noise, but may ignore useful exploratory signals unless dashboards and logs remain available.

lenses.name: Structured Event Logs
lenses.focus: Treat logs as a typed diagnostic event stream with stable fields, correlation keys, and intentional verbosity boundaries.
concerns: Canonical log schema for actor, tenant, request, operation, resource, outcome, duration, and correlation identifiers
Explicit event names instead of prose-only messages for machine search and aggregation
Log-level policy that separates audit facts, operator diagnostics, expected denials, and true faults
Redaction and omission rules for secrets, credentials, personal data, and high-volume payloads
Cardinality control for labels and fields that may explode storage or query cost
Sampling or suppression policy for hot loops, retries, polling, and expected validation failures

signals: debugging depends on reproducing issues locally because production logs are ambiguous
incidents require manual grep across free-form messages
multi-tenant or request-scoped failures lack reliable correlation identifiers
operators need to distinguish expected business rejections from system faults
log volume is high but low-value or expensive to query

lenses.tension: Improves forensic detail but can increase implementation noise, storage cost, privacy exposure, and schema maintenance burden.

lenses.name: Telemetry Safety Governance
lenses.focus: Constrain observability so it remains compliant, affordable, tenant-safe, and usable under real production volume.
concerns: PII, secrets, credentials, tokens, payloads, and customer data redaction at instrumentation boundaries
Tenant isolation in logs, traces, metrics, exports, dashboards, and support tooling
Retention, access control, audit, and deletion policy for telemetry data
Sampling, aggregation, and cardinality limits that preserve signal while controlling cost
Failure behavior when telemetry exporters, collectors, or backends are slow or unavailable
Review process for new fields that could expose sensitive data or create high-cardinality explosions

signals: observability data may contain regulated, customer, or tenant-sensitive information
telemetry cost is rising faster than traffic or storage budgets
teams add ad hoc fields to logs or spans without review
operators need production diagnostics but cannot safely expose raw payloads
telemetry backend failures have affected application availability

lenses.tension: Protects users and operating budgets but can reduce diagnostic fidelity and slow down instrumentation work.

lenses.name: Trace Context Propagation
lenses.focus: Make causality visible across services, threads, queues, callbacks, and retries through disciplined trace and span boundaries.
concerns: Propagation of trace identifiers through HTTP, RPC, messaging, scheduled jobs, and async continuations
Span naming that reflects stable operations rather than dynamic resource names
Parent-child relationships for fan-out, retries, background work, and deferred callbacks
Baggage policy for tenant, actor, request class, and other contextual fields
Sampling strategy that preserves failed, slow, rare, and high-value traces
Joining logs, metrics, and reports to traces without duplicating sensitive payloads

signals: requests cross multiple services or queues and failures vanish between boundaries
latency investigations cannot identify the slow dependency or fan-out branch
retry storms, timeout layering, or async callbacks obscure root cause
operators need to follow one customer action through multiple subsystems
a migration introduces distributed ownership or remote dependencies

lenses.tension: Reveals distributed causality but adds propagation plumbing, sampling choices, and overhead that may distract from simpler single-process diagnostics.

lenses.name: Typed Result Reports
lenses.focus: Return durable result objects that explain what happened, what was skipped, what failed, and what the caller or operator should do next.
concerns: Result types that encode success, partial success, no-op, validation failure, conflict, retryable failure, and permanent failure
Counts, identifiers, timings, decisions, skipped items, warnings, and follow-up actions for batch or reconciliation operations
Separation between stable public result contracts and richer internal diagnostic reports
Aggregation of per-item failures without losing causality or overloading exceptions
Serialization shape suitable for CLI output, API responses, audit records, and operator dashboards
Compatibility policy for report fields consumed by automation or support workflows

signals: callers need to know more than success or exception
batch or sync jobs can partially complete and require follow-up
support teams ask engineers to inspect databases to explain an operation outcome
APIs return vague errors despite making important internal decisions
automation needs stable machine-readable summaries

lenses.tension: Improves caller ergonomics and post-run analysis but can harden internal details into public contracts too early.

lenses.name: Async Boundaries
lenses.focus: Owns the distinction between nonblocking I/O, CPU parallelism, cancellation, and bounded fan-out so async code improves throughput without overload.
concerns: blocking calls hidden inside async paths
unbounded task creation, fan-out, queue growth, and retry storms
cancellation propagation, deadlines, and cleanup semantics
thread pool starvation and sync-over-async deadlocks
structured concurrency ownership for child work

signals: request handlers call many remote services
timeouts leave background work running
throughput collapses under concurrent load
code mixes blocking locks or I/O with async APIs
fan-out count scales with input size or tenant count

lenses.tension: Adds lifecycle and cancellation complexity; may reduce simple sequential clarity even when the bottleneck is elsewhere.

lenses.name: Batch Amortization
lenses.focus: Owns batching and coalescing where fixed per-operation cost dominates, while keeping latency, fairness, and memory bounds explicit.
concerns: flush triggers based on size, time, pressure, and shutdown
deduplication and coalescing of equivalent work
batch size tuning for throughput, latency, and downstream limits
partial failure handling and retry granularity
fairness across tenants, priorities, and small requests

signals: many tiny operations pay repeated network, syscall, transaction, or serialization cost
downstream APIs support bulk operations
throughput is low despite low CPU utilization
logs show duplicate work queued close together
per-item commits or flushes dominate runtime

lenses.tension: Improves throughput by waiting or accumulating work; can hurt tail latency, memory footprint, fairness, and error isolation.

lenses.name: Cache Economics
lenses.focus: Owns caching only where hit rate, invalidation, stampede control, and memory budget make the cache cheaper than recomputation or refetch.
concerns: cache key design, cardinality, tenant isolation, and negative caching
TTL, invalidation, freshness, and stale-read tolerance
stampede protection, request coalescing, and warmup strategy
bounded size, eviction policy, and memory accounting
local versus shared cache placement and consistency cost

signals: same expensive read or computation repeats frequently
remote dependency latency dominates profiles
database or API load is driven by duplicate lookups
cache exists but hit rate, cardinality, or invalidation is unknown
traffic bursts cause cache stampedes

lenses.tension: Buys speed with freshness risk, memory use, invalidation complexity, and possible hidden coupling to data ownership.

lenses.name: Contention Control
lenses.focus: Owns shared-state pressure: shrink critical sections, remove global bottlenecks, and make concurrency scale predictably.
concerns: coarse locks, synchronized caches, global registries, and singleton queues
atomic hot spots, compare-and-swap retry loops, and false sharing
lock ordering, priority inversion, and convoy effects
partitioned ownership, actors, sharding, or immutable snapshots
critical-section I/O, logging, allocation, and callback hazards

signals: CPU is available but throughput does not increase with concurrency
profiles show lock wait, monitor contention, or atomic retry cost
one shared cache, queue, map, or counter sits on every request path
parallel workers spend time waiting instead of doing useful work
tail latency spikes during bursts

lenses.tension: May replace simple shared state with partitioned ownership or immutable snapshots that increase design complexity and memory use.

lenses.name: Data Access Shape
lenses.focus: Owns query and remote-call shape so data is fetched in intentional batches, projections, and joins rather than accidental N+1 paths.
concerns: N+1 database queries, RPC calls, filesystem stats, and per-row authorization checks
projection width, selectivity, indexes, and pagination boundaries
eager versus lazy loading based on measured access patterns
batch APIs, prefetch plans, and deduplicated lookups
round-trip count across service, database, and storage boundaries

signals: latency grows linearly with result count
logs show repeated similar queries or calls per request
list or search endpoints slow down on realistic datasets
ORM lazy loading or resolver code hides data fetches
remote calls happen inside loops

lenses.tension: Can make access code less generic and couple callers to data shape; aggressive prefetching may overread or buffer too much.

lenses.name: Hot-Path Allocation
lenses.focus: Owns allocation discipline on tight loops and high-volume request paths where object churn becomes latency or GC pressure.
concerns: temporary collections, closures, boxing, reflection metadata, and per-item adapters
copy-heavy parsing, formatting, serialization, and transformation stages
allocation-free fast paths with clear fallback paths
pooling only where ownership, clearing, and contention are safe
avoiding micro-optimizations outside measured hot paths

signals: allocation rate is high under normal load
GC pauses or heap churn dominate profiles
tight loops process large collections or streams
small request payloads trigger many short-lived objects
hot code builds intermediate arrays, maps, or strings

lenses.tension: Trades readability and API simplicity for lower churn; object pooling can introduce lifecycle bugs and contention if overused.

lenses.name: Memory Lifetime
lenses.focus: Owns retained memory: make object lifetimes, buffer ownership, and cardinality bounds explicit so footprint stays stable under real load.
concerns: unbounded maps, queues, caches, registries, and in-flight request state
retention through closures, listeners, contexts, thread locals, and metrics labels
large object fragmentation, buffer reuse, and copy chains
tenant, session, and batch cardinality limits
memory behavior during retries, errors, and cancellation

signals: resident memory grows with uptime or tenant count
heap snapshots show unexpected retainers
large batches or concurrent requests trigger OOM
caches or queues have no explicit size budget
high-cardinality labels or keys appear in runtime structures

lenses.tension: Forces bounds and ownership rules that may reject convenient global state, broad caching, or all-at-once APIs.

lenses.name: Parallel Partitioning
lenses.focus: Owns CPU-bound parallelism: split work into chunks that amortize scheduling overhead while preserving locality and deterministic results.
concerns: task granularity, work stealing, and scheduler overhead
partitioning by key, range, shard, or affinity to avoid shared writes
deterministic reduction and merge phases
data locality, cache friendliness, and false sharing
fallback behavior on small inputs or constrained cores

signals: single-core CPU saturation limits throughput
large independent workloads are processed sequentially
batch jobs miss wall-clock deadlines
profiles show pure computation rather than I/O wait
parallel rewrite is proposed without partitioning rules

lenses.tension: Improves throughput at the cost of scheduling overhead, harder debugging, nondeterminism risk, and possible worse latency for small workloads.

lenses.name: Budgeted Profiling
lenses.focus: Owns measurement first: define explicit latency, throughput, allocation, and memory budgets before choosing optimizations.
concerns: credible baseline benchmarks for the user-visible hot paths
p50, p95, p99, throughput, allocation rate, and resident memory targets
profiling evidence before speculative rewrites
regression gates for performance-sensitive code paths
representative data size, concurrency level, and deployment topology

signals: performance goals are vague or disputed
optimization proposals are not tied to measurements
recent change claims speedup without benchmark evidence
latency or memory regressions are hard to reproduce
team needs a guardrail against future slowdowns

lenses.tension: Slows immediate implementation in exchange for evidence; can reject attractive optimizations that do not move the agreed budget.

lenses.name: Wire Efficiency
lenses.focus: Owns payload and serialization cost so services exchange only necessary data with formats and encoders suited to the hot path.
concerns: payload projection, field sparsity, pagination, and overfetching
serialization CPU cost, allocation rate, and reflection overhead
compression thresholds and CPU-versus-bandwidth tradeoffs
incremental decoding and zero-copy or low-copy transfer where practical
schema evolution without forcing wasteful compatibility payloads

signals: profiles show serialization, parsing, or compression near the top
network payloads are large relative to useful fields
mobile, edge, or cross-region clients pay high transfer cost
APIs return broad objects for narrow use cases
large messages cause buffering or memory spikes

lenses.tension: Can introduce format complexity, compatibility work, and specialized code paths that reduce generic API ergonomics.

lenses.name: Streaming Backpressure
lenses.focus: Owns streaming pipelines where bounded buffers, chunking, and flow control avoid loading whole datasets or overwhelming downstream consumers.
concerns: streaming versus buffering boundaries and where materialization is unavoidable
chunk size, cursor lifetime, and partial result semantics
producer-consumer backpressure and bounded queues
incremental parsing, encoding, hashing, compression, and upload/download
cleanup on cancellation, early termination, and downstream failure

signals: code loads full files, result sets, or payloads into memory
large exports, imports, reports, or migrations hit memory limits
slow consumers cause queue growth
partial results would improve perceived latency
workflows need cancellation while processing large data

lenses.tension: Streaming improves footprint and responsiveness but complicates retries, transactions, validation, ordering, and error reporting.

lenses.name: Tail Latency
lenses.focus: Owns p95 and p99 behavior by analyzing queues, head-of-line blocking, overload policy, and fairness rather than average throughput.
concerns: queue depth, wait time, service time, and utilization per stage
head-of-line blocking from large requests, locks, batches, and shared pools
timeouts, deadlines, admission control, and load shedding
priority, fairness, and noisy-neighbor isolation
cold starts, cache misses, and retry amplification during bursts

signals: average latency looks acceptable but users see spikes
bursty traffic causes slow recovery
one slow request blocks unrelated work
retries or background jobs compete with foreground traffic
SLOs are defined on p95 or p99 latency

lenses.tension: May sacrifice peak throughput or batching efficiency to protect worst-case latency and fairness.

lenses.name: Backpressure & Load Shedding
lenses.focus: Make overload explicit by bounding queues, limiting concurrency, and shedding low-value work before saturation corrupts all work.
concerns: bounded queues with clear reject, drop, coalesce, or defer semantics
adaptive concurrency limits, rate limits, and admission control by tenant or workload class
priority policies that protect critical traffic over bulk, optional, or retry traffic
producer backpressure signals for streaming, queues, APIs, and batch pipelines
load-shedding responses that are actionable for callers and observable for operators

signals: system can receive traffic faster than it can process
queues are unbounded or memory grows during dependency slowness
background jobs, streams, or webhooks accumulate backlog during incidents
product has mixed-priority traffic sharing the same capacity

lenses.tension: Preserves system health by refusing or delaying work, which conflicts with naive success-rate goals and unlimited intake.

lenses.name: Chaos & Fault Injection
lenses.focus: Convert resilience claims into tests that inject latency, errors, drops, duplicates, restarts, and partial commits.
concerns: fault-injection coverage for timeouts, retries, circuit opening, stale fallbacks, and load shedding
deterministic tests for duplicate messages, reordered events, process crashes, and ambiguous commits
load and soak scenarios that prove backpressure and queue bounds under overload
contract tests for dependency error mapping and retry eligibility
safe production-game-day scope with abort criteria and measurable hypotheses

signals: design claims resilience but only tests happy paths
failure behavior depends on timing, concurrency, queues, or external dependencies
release has high blast radius or follows a reliability incident
team needs confidence in retry, idempotency, breaker, or recovery behavior

lenses.tension: Requires test harness investment and controlled fault scenarios, trading delivery speed for evidence that failure behavior works.

lenses.name: Circuit Breakers & Bulkheads
lenses.focus: Contain dependency failure by isolating capacity pools and opening circuits before one unhealthy path consumes the whole system.
concerns: breaker thresholds, half-open probes, cooldown windows, and failure classification
separate concurrency, thread, connection, and queue pools for risky dependencies
fallback behavior while a circuit is open and how callers learn the degraded state
avoidance of shared global pools that let one tenant, feature, or dependency starve others
operator controls for forced open, forced closed, and live threshold tuning

signals: one dependency outage can exhaust shared worker or connection pools
system calls slow or unreliable third-party APIs
multi-tenant workloads need noisy-neighbor isolation
incidents show cascading failure from a single degraded downstream

lenses.tension: Protects the broader system by deliberately failing some calls, which can reduce apparent availability for the isolated path.

lenses.name: Dependency Topology Isolation
lenses.focus: Shape service and dependency topology so failures remain local, optional paths stay optional, and critical paths have fewer moving parts.
concerns: critical-path dependency count and fan-out reduction
sync versus async boundaries for non-critical work
regional, zonal, tenant, and provider isolation assumptions
dependency tiering by availability requirement and blast radius
startup, deployment, and configuration dependencies that can prevent recovery

signals: a user request fans out across many services or providers
optional enrichment is called inline on critical paths
service startup fails when non-critical dependencies are unavailable
architecture review is deciding between sync calls, events, queues, and caches

lenses.tension: May add asynchronous workflows and duplicated read models to reduce runtime coupling.

lenses.name: Failure Mode Inventory
lenses.focus: Force the design to enumerate realistic failure modes first, then make every mitigation trace back to a named failure.
concerns: dependency outages, partial failures, slow responses, malformed responses, and quota exhaustion
local failures such as disk full, memory pressure, process restarts, clock skew, and configuration drift
distinguishing transient, permanent, overload, consistency, and operator-induced failures
explicit owner for each mitigation, fallback, alert, and manual recovery path
failure assumptions documented at API, job, queue, storage, and external service boundaries

signals: design mentions retries or fallbacks without saying which failures they handle
system integrates with multiple external services or queues
incident history includes unknown root causes or surprising cascading failures
requirements include high availability, disaster recovery, or operational handoff

lenses.tension: Adds explicit modeling and review overhead before implementation, which can feel heavy for narrow low-risk changes.

lenses.name: Fallbacks & Staleness
lenses.focus: Use caches, replicas, defaults, and alternate providers only with explicit freshness, correctness, and invalidation rules.
concerns: maximum staleness per data type, caller, and business decision
cache-aside, read-through, replica, snapshot, and static-default fallback tradeoffs
negative caching, stale-if-error behavior, and cache stampede protection
fallback provenance in responses, logs, metrics, and audit trails
safe invalidation and warm-up behavior after provider recovery

signals: read availability can be improved with stale or approximate data
dependency outages should not block browse, search, recommendations, or reports
system already has caches but no explicit stale-data contract
multiple providers or replicas can serve similar but not identical data

lenses.tension: Increases availability for reads but risks decisions based on stale or lower-fidelity data.

lenses.name: Graceful Degradation Contracts
lenses.focus: Define which capabilities may degrade, what users or callers receive, and which invariants must never degrade.
concerns: critical versus optional features, fields, recommendations, enrichments, and background side effects
degraded response schemas, stale data markers, partial-result semantics, and caller-visible warnings
business invariants that require hard failure instead of degraded success
UX, API, and batch behavior when dependencies are unavailable or stale
restoration behavior after degradation ends, including catch-up and user communication

signals: feature combines mandatory core behavior with optional enrichment
current errors could be replaced by stale cache, partial results, or queued work
consumer contracts do not distinguish complete, partial, stale, and failed outcomes
requirements prioritize availability but still need correctness boundaries

lenses.tension: Can improve availability while increasing semantic complexity for callers and support teams.

lenses.name: Idempotency & Deduplication
lenses.focus: Design every retried or replayed side effect around stable operation identity, duplicate suppression, and deterministic outcomes.
concerns: idempotency key generation, scoping, retention, expiry, and collision behavior
deduplication stores and uniqueness constraints at the actual side-effect boundary
response replay semantics for duplicate successful and duplicate in-flight requests
consumer-side dedupe for at-least-once queues, webhooks, CDC, and scheduled jobs
safe handling of partial commits, process crashes, and ambiguous remote outcomes

signals: operations create, charge, notify, mutate inventory, or trigger irreversible side effects
message delivery is at-least-once or webhook callbacks can be repeated
clients may retry after timeout without knowing whether the first attempt committed
migration or backfill may replay historical operations

lenses.tension: Requires durable state and stricter operation identity, trading implementation simplicity for correctness under replay.

lenses.name: Recovery & Reconciliation
lenses.focus: Assume partial failure will happen and design deterministic repair loops that converge state after retries, replays, and outages.
concerns: checkpointing, watermarks, replay ranges, and safe restart behavior
repair jobs for orphaned records, missed events, duplicate side effects, and divergent replicas
idempotent reconciliation functions with bounded scans and audit output
manual override paths when automated repair cannot decide safely
success criteria for convergence, backlog drain, and data correctness after incident recovery

signals: workflow spans multiple stores, services, queues, or external systems
partial commits or lost callbacks can leave durable inconsistency
data migration, backfill, or event processing needs restartability
operators currently fix failed workflows with ad hoc scripts

lenses.tension: Accepts eventual repair over immediate atomicity, which may be unacceptable for strict real-time invariants.

lenses.name: Recovery Runbook Design
lenses.focus: The catalog covers failure modes and repair loops, but lacks the operator-facing procedure lens for pause, drain, replay, rollback, escalation, and verification.

lenses.name: Resilience SLO Feedback
lenses.focus: Tie resilience mechanisms to service-level objectives, health signals, and decision metrics so operators know when protections help or hurt.
concerns: SLOs for availability, latency, durability, backlog age, freshness, and recovery time
metrics for retries, timeouts, open circuits, fallback use, shed load, and duplicate suppression
health and readiness checks that reflect dependency criticality and degraded modes
error taxonomy separating caller error, dependency failure, overload, timeout, and invariant violation
dashboards and alerts that reveal resilience mechanism saturation before user impact

signals: resilience behavior exists but operators cannot tell when it activates
health checks report healthy while users see degraded or failed operations
alerts fire on symptoms but not on retry storms, open circuits, or backlog growth
SLOs or incident response requirements are part of the work

lenses.tension: Adds telemetry and operational contracts that may be viewed as non-functional scope but are necessary to validate resilience behavior.

lenses.name: Retry Safety Policy
lenses.focus: Make retries conditional, bounded, jittered, and safe for the operation semantics instead of a generic wrapper around failure.
concerns: retry eligibility by error class, idempotency guarantee, and caller deadline
bounded attempts with exponential backoff, jitter, and retry budget enforcement
separate policies for reads, writes, async jobs, polling, and bulk operations
avoidance of retry storms during overload or regional dependency incidents
surfacing final failure with enough context to diagnose exhausted retry policy

signals: code retries all exceptions or all 5xx responses uniformly
write paths call external systems or payment-like side effects
traffic spikes or outages could make clients retry in synchronized waves
requirements mention transient failures, flaky networks, or distributed workers

lenses.tension: Improves transient-failure success but can amplify load and hide systemic faults if not paired with budgets and shedding.

lenses.name: Timeout Budget Contracts
lenses.focus: Treat timeouts as end-to-end latency budgets with explicit propagation, cancellation, and ownership at every boundary.
concerns: per-hop timeout allocation from caller deadline instead of independent magic numbers
cancellation propagation across HTTP, RPC, database, queue, and background work
connect, read, write, acquire-lock, and total operation timeout separation
default timeout policy for new clients, jobs, and SDK surfaces
behavior when work finishes after the caller has already given up

signals: requests can fan out to several dependencies
current design lacks deadlines or uses framework defaults
incidents include stuck workers, exhausted connection pools, or long-tail latency
user-facing flows have strict latency or availability SLOs

lenses.tension: Can reject slow-but-eventually-successful work earlier, trading completion rate for bounded resource use and predictable latency.

lenses.name: Transaction & Compensation Boundaries
lenses.focus: Draw hard boundaries around what is atomic, what is eventually consistent, and what needs a compensating action.
concerns: local transaction scope versus remote calls, queues, and side effects
outbox, inbox, saga, and compensation patterns for cross-boundary workflows
ordering guarantees and exactly-once illusions across services and brokers
durable intent records before irreversible side effects
operator-safe compensation for refunds, reversals, cancels, and cleanup

signals: workflow updates local state and calls external systems in one logical operation
design relies on distributed transactions or assumes exactly-once delivery
side effects must be reversed when later steps fail
business process spans services, queues, or human approval steps

lenses.tension: Introduces workflow state and compensation complexity instead of pretending cross-system atomicity is available.

lenses.name: Abstraction Boundary Fit
lenses.focus: Validate that a proposed shared abstraction groups code with the same semantics, ownership, lifecycle, and change cadence.
concerns: semantic cohesion across all intended consumers
whether callers share invariants or only similar syntax
ownership and maintenance responsibility for the abstraction
change cadence mismatch between consumers
leaky parameters that expose consumer-specific details
contract tests proving substitutability

signals: a helper, base class, or shared package is being introduced
similar code appears in multiple modules owned by different teams
callers need reuse but have different domain rules
an abstraction already requires caller-specific flags or hooks

lenses.tension: Trades implementation speed and apparent consolidation for stricter proof that reuse is semantically real.

lenses.name: Composition Seam Design
lenses.focus: Replace brittle inheritance trees with explicit strategy, policy, adapter, or component seams.
concerns: deep class hierarchies and fragile base classes
template methods with override-order hazards
subclass explosion from behavior combinations
state ownership across composed components
small interfaces that model real variation points
test seams for injected behavior

signals: new behavior requires another subclass
callers override protected methods to change small policy decisions
base classes contain optional hooks or no-op methods
behavior combinations are growing faster than concrete types

lenses.tension: Improves substitutability and testing at the cost of more explicit wiring and more named collaborators.

lenses.name: Configuration vs Composition
lenses.focus: Detect abstractions that hide divergent behavior behind flags and replace them with explicit composed parts.
concerns: boolean arguments and option bags that control major behavior
invalid or untested configuration combinations
defaults that encode one consumer's assumptions
conditional branches selected by caller identity
type-safe construction of valid behavior combinations
discoverability of behavior through named components

signals: a shared function or class has many flags or mode parameters
new consumers require special-case configuration
tests cover only a few combinations of many options
callers must know internal behavior to set options correctly

lenses.tension: Favors more named types and explicit assembly over one highly configurable entry point.

lenses.name: Dependency Direction Reuse
lenses.focus: Ensure reuse does not create cycles, layer leaks, or unstable dependencies pointing into application-specific code.
concerns: import cycles introduced by shared utilities
lower layers depending on higher-level workflows
framework details leaking into domain abstractions
shared packages that depend on one consumer
adapter placement around reusable core logic
stable dependency principles across modules

signals: a helper is moved to a common module but imports application code
layering rules are weakened to avoid duplication
shared code pulls in heavy framework or infrastructure dependencies
multiple services reuse logic by depending on each other

lenses.tension: Can require duplicate adapters or thin wrappers to preserve clean dependency flow.

lenses.name: Domain Language DRYness
lenses.focus: Unify duplicated domain concepts and business rules before unifying incidental implementation structure.
concerns: same business rule implemented under different names
validation or authorization logic duplicated across workflows
domain terms that drift between modules
value objects and policies that encode shared meaning
places where syntactic similarity hides different concepts
user-visible consistency of decisions and error states

signals: similar rules are enforced in frontend, backend, jobs, or integrations
defects come from one workflow accepting what another rejects
teams use different names for the same domain concept
reuse debates mix business duplication with code-shape duplication

lenses.tension: May preserve technical duplication while forcing sharper domain modeling and rule ownership.

lenses.name: Generic Abstraction Pressure Test
lenses.focus: Challenge generic abstractions until they prove enough real call sites, stable variation axes, and deletion value.
concerns: number of real consumers versus hypothetical future users
explicit variation axes backed by current requirements
configuration parameters added for imagined cases
negative examples the abstraction must intentionally not support
migration cost if the abstraction is wrong
ability to delete or inline the abstraction later

signals: a reusable framework is proposed before the second or third concrete use
arguments rely on future-proofing rather than current requirements
a helper has many type parameters, options, or callbacks from the start
the team is abstracting while requirements are still volatile

lenses.tension: May delay reusable infrastructure and force short-term duplication until the shape of reuse is better evidenced.

lenses.name: Shared Kernel Ownership
lenses.focus: Treat shared code as a governed product with clear ownership, compatibility rules, and release discipline.
concerns: single accountable owner for shared modules
semantic versioning or compatibility expectations
consumer migration and deprecation paths
dependency direction into and out of the shared kernel
review gates for adding new shared concepts
release cadence mismatch across consumers

signals: multiple teams depend on a common package or module
shared types are changed for one consumer and break others
a monorepo has common code without ownership boundaries
domain models or validation rules are reused across services

lenses.tension: Adds governance overhead and can slow individual feature teams to protect cross-consumer stability.

lenses.name: Test Reuse Without Obscurity
lenses.focus: Reuse test contracts, builders, and fixtures only where they improve coverage without making failures harder to diagnose.
concerns: opaque shared fixtures that hide important setup
contract tests for reusable interfaces and adapters
test data builders with explicit domain intent
over-generalized assertion helpers
failure messages that identify the violated rule
local readability versus shared setup reduction

signals: test setup is repetitive and expensive to maintain
shared fixtures make unrelated tests fail together
a new interface or strategy needs consistent behavioral guarantees
reviewers cannot tell what a test scenario is proving

lenses.tension: Allows verbose local tests when shared helpers would reduce diagnostic value.

lenses.name: Abuse Resistance Controls
lenses.focus: Owns controls for malicious but valid-looking use, including enumeration, scraping, brute force, resource exhaustion, and workflow abuse.
concerns: rate limits, quotas, backoff, lockouts, and progressive challenges
identifier enumeration, credential stuffing, invite abuse, spam, and mass assignment
expensive queries, fanout actions, uploads, exports, and notification storms
tenant-level fairness and per-user, per-IP, per-token, and per-resource limits
safe degradation, retry semantics, and abuse telemetry

signals: public endpoint performs search, login, signup, password reset, invite, upload, export, or notification work
attackers can cheaply trigger expensive compute, storage, network, or third-party cost
sequential or guessable identifiers expose user, tenant, or business data
workflow has no quotas, cool-downs, or abuse-specific observability

lenses.tension: Introduces friction and false-positive handling that may conflict with growth, support, and legitimate high-volume use.

lenses.name: Audit Forensics Readiness
lenses.focus: Owns whether security-relevant actions can be reconstructed without logging secrets or creating a new sensitive data liability.
concerns: audit events for auth, authorization decisions, privileged actions, exports, config changes, and secret access
tamper resistance, timestamp consistency, actor attribution, correlation IDs, and retention
redaction of tokens, passwords, PII, payloads, and third-party credentials
alertable signals for abuse, policy bypass, suspicious automation, and data exfiltration
incident response queries, evidence preservation, and customer-facing audit trails

signals: feature changes permissions, secrets, billing, data exports, admin controls, or compliance-relevant state
incident investigation would require reconstructing who did what and why
logs currently include raw payloads or omit actor and resource identifiers
customers, regulators, or internal security need audit evidence

lenses.tension: Adds storage, privacy, and schema governance work, and can compete with prevention-focused security reviews.

lenses.name: Object Authorization Boundaries
lenses.focus: Owns authorization at the resource and action level, especially where tenant, ownership, role, or state boundaries can be bypassed.
concerns: object-level access checks for read, write, delete, export, and admin actions
tenant scoping in queries, caches, search indexes, events, and background jobs
confused deputy risks in service-to-service and delegated operations
state-dependent authorization such as draft, approved, locked, or archived
deny-by-default behavior for new actions and unknown resource types

signals: multi-tenant data or user-owned resources are accessed by identifiers
new roles, permissions, sharing, delegation, or admin workflows are added
queries are built from client-provided IDs or filters
background jobs act on behalf of users or tenants

lenses.tension: Favors centralized policy and explicit checks, which may compete with simpler local code paths and delivery speed.

lenses.name: Crypto Data Protection
lenses.focus: Owns confidentiality and integrity protections for sensitive data by requiring proven primitives, explicit key ownership, and clear data classification.
concerns: data classification, retention, masking, tokenization, and field-level sensitivity
encryption in transit, encryption at rest, signing, hashing, and password storage
nonce, salt, IV, randomness, algorithm agility, and key separation
backups, analytics copies, exports, logs, and support access to sensitive data
regulatory and contractual data handling boundaries

signals: feature stores or transmits credentials, PII, payment data, health data, private content, or customer secrets
design proposes custom cryptography, reversible tokens, hashes, signatures, or encryption
data is copied into logs, analytics, search, backups, exports, or support tools
cross-region, cross-tenant, or third-party data sharing is introduced

lenses.tension: May add key management, query limitations, and data handling constraints that complicate product analytics and operations.

lenses.name: Parser Deserialization Safety
lenses.focus: Owns safe handling of complex formats by treating parsers, archives, object graphs, and file processors as attackable code execution surfaces.
concerns: unsafe object deserialization, polymorphic type loading, and gadget chains
archive traversal, decompression bombs, nested payloads, and parser resource limits
file type confusion, MIME sniffing, magic bytes, and extension mismatches
XML entity expansion, schema resolution, and external resource loading
sandboxing and isolation for document, image, media, and model processors

signals: system accepts uploads, imports, archives, serialized objects, XML, YAML, PDFs, images, or media
parsing uses plugins, reflection, class names, custom codecs, or auto type resolution
large or nested payloads can be processed synchronously
file processing runs with broad filesystem, network, or credential access

lenses.tension: Adds format restrictions, resource caps, and isolation that can reduce format compatibility and processing throughput.

lenses.name: Developer Secure Defaults
lenses.focus: Owns security ergonomics by making the safest path the easiest path for future contributors and downstream integrators.
concerns: framework helpers that centralize auth, validation, escaping, secret access, and logging redaction
default-deny APIs, typed capabilities, safe constructors, and impossible invalid states
tests, linters, scanners, templates, and CI checks for recurring security mistakes
documentation that names mandatory security hooks without leaking implementation secrets
migration paths that remove unsafe legacy affordances instead of preserving them indefinitely

signals: library, framework, SDK, template, or platform code will be reused by many teams
same security check appears in many handlers, services, or generated files
new contributors can accidentally bypass auth, validation, escaping, or redaction
security guidance exists only in docs or review comments

lenses.tension: Invests in platform shape and guardrails, which can delay a one-off feature and may constrain advanced escape hatches.

lenses.name: Identity Session Integrity
lenses.focus: Owns authentication correctness from credential proof through session propagation so the system never confuses who is acting.
concerns: credential verification, MFA, reauthentication, and recovery flows
session fixation, replay, token lifetime, rotation, and revocation
JWT, cookie, API key, and service token audience and issuer checks
identity propagation across services, queues, and async jobs
account linking, impersonation, delegation, and break-glass paths

signals: new login, SSO, API key, token exchange, or account recovery flow
sessions cross domains, devices, services, queues, or background jobs
design introduces impersonation, delegation, admin assist, or machine identity
authentication state is cached or embedded in long-lived tokens

lenses.tension: Pushes for stricter session semantics and shorter lifetimes, which can increase user friction and operational complexity.

lenses.name: Injection Sink Elimination
lenses.focus: Owns the elimination of string-built execution contexts by forcing parameterization, structured APIs, and explicit escaping at every sink.
concerns: SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, shell, template, XPath, regex, and expression-language sinks
HTML, Markdown, rich text, CSV, log, and header injection contexts
dynamic query builders, sort keys, filter DSLs, and report generators
server-side request forgery through URL fetchers and metadata endpoints
stored injection through databases, queues, caches, exports, and notifications

signals: untrusted data is inserted into queries, commands, templates, documents, or URLs
feature adds search, filtering, formulas, scripting, rendering, or export
code constructs strings for interpreters or external systems
sanitization is caller-managed instead of enforced by the sink API

lenses.tension: Pushes teams toward less flexible but safer structured APIs, which can constrain advanced query and templating features.

lenses.name: Canonical Input Gate
lenses.focus: Owns the rule that untrusted input is parsed, normalized, constrained, and typed once at the boundary before business logic sees it.
concerns: schema validation, allowlists, length limits, numeric bounds, and enum handling
canonicalization of paths, URLs, Unicode, encodings, locale-sensitive text, and timestamps
ambiguous null, empty, default, duplicate, and unknown field semantics
validation parity across UI, API, workers, imports, and internal callers
safe error messages that do not leak parser or policy internals

signals: feature accepts user text, files, URLs, filters, expressions, metadata, or imports
multiple entry points can create or mutate the same domain object
the design depends on path, host, redirect, email, phone, date, or locale parsing
validation currently lives deep inside business logic or varies by caller

lenses.tension: May reject previously tolerated data and force compatibility decisions before product behavior is fully settled.

lenses.name: Least Privilege Execution
lenses.focus: Owns runtime blast-radius reduction by ensuring every component has only the permissions, network reachability, and data access it actually needs.
concerns: service accounts, IAM policies, database grants, filesystem access, and cloud roles
network egress, metadata service access, admin endpoints, and internal control planes
separation of read, write, migration, admin, and background job privileges
container, function, worker, and plugin sandbox boundaries
privilege escalation paths through queues, schedulers, caches, and deployment automation

signals: new service, worker, deployment target, plugin host, or automation account is added
component can reach broad internal networks, cloud APIs, databases, or object stores
one compromised workload could mutate production data or infrastructure
same credential is reused across environments, tenants, or job types

lenses.tension: Can increase policy count, deployment complexity, and operational debugging effort.

lenses.name: Privacy Data Minimization
lenses.focus: Security coverage is strong, but privacy-specific purpose limitation, retention, consent, deletion, and minimization are not equivalent to encryption or auditability.

lenses.name: Secrets Key Lifecycle
lenses.focus: Owns secrets from creation through rotation and revocation so credentials are never hardcoded, overexposed, logged, or impossible to replace.
concerns: secret generation, storage, envelope encryption, rotation, and revocation paths
environment variables, build logs, crash dumps, telemetry, traces, and support bundles
API keys, signing keys, database passwords, OAuth secrets, and webhook secrets
secret scope, audience, expiration, and blast radius
local development, CI, test fixtures, and incident replacement procedures

signals: design introduces credentials, API keys, signing tokens, certificates, or webhooks
service integrates with third-party APIs, payment providers, identity providers, or cloud resources
secrets are needed in CI, local development, logs, workers, or generated artifacts
rotation or emergency revocation has no clear owner or path

lenses.tension: Demands operational machinery and failure handling that can feel heavy for prototypes or low-risk internal tools.

lenses.name: Supply Chain Provenance
lenses.focus: Owns dependency, build, artifact, and deployment integrity so untrusted code cannot enter production unnoticed.
concerns: dependency pinning, lockfiles, transitive risk, typosquatting, and abandoned packages
build script execution, code generation, native extensions, and postinstall hooks
artifact signing, provenance, reproducibility, and promotion between environments
CI permissions, third-party actions, package registries, and release credentials
vulnerability triage policy, exception expiry, and patch rollout ownership

signals: project adds dependencies, plugins, generators, build steps, CI actions, or package publishing
software is distributed to customers or deployed through automated pipelines
dependency updates are unreviewed, unpinned, or mixed with feature work
build pipeline can access production secrets or write release artifacts

lenses.tension: Prioritizes controlled inputs and reproducible builds, which may slow dependency adoption and release velocity.

lenses.name: Trust Boundary Map
lenses.focus: Owns the system attack surface by making every actor, data flow, trust transition, and privileged operation explicit before design choices harden.
concerns: externally reachable endpoints, jobs, hooks, queues, and callbacks
implicit trust in internal services, headers, metadata, or client state
privilege transitions between user, service, admin, and automation contexts
untrusted-to-trusted data flow through storage, caches, logs, and events
missing abuse cases for high-value actions

signals: new public API, webhook, worker, agent, plugin, or integration boundary
multiple actors or tenants interact with shared state
design relies on internal network trust or undocumented caller assumptions
privileged actions are reachable through indirect paths

lenses.tension: Spends time broadening the model and may slow teams that already need a narrow implementation review.

lenses.name: Additive Schema Evolution
lenses.focus: Prefer expand-only schema and message evolution so old readers, new readers, old writers, and new writers can coexist safely.
concerns: optional fields with clear defaults and absence semantics
tolerant readers and conservative writers
enum, union, and discriminator extension rules
field reservation, renaming avoidance, and tombstoned identifiers
unknown-field preservation where round-tripping matters
schema registry or compatibility mode configuration

signals: the change adds, removes, renames, or retypes fields
events or records are consumed asynchronously by unknown readers
deployments are rolling or clients upgrade slowly
schema validation currently rejects unknown future data

lenses.tension: Can leave awkward legacy fields and indirections in place longer than designers would like.

lenses.name: Behavioral Compatibility Invariants
lenses.focus: Protect the observable behaviors users rely on, including defaults, ordering, idempotency, errors, timing, and edge-case semantics, not just type signatures.
concerns: default value and omitted-parameter behavior
error codes, exception types, retryability, and message contracts
ordering, pagination, filtering, and idempotency guarantees
performance promises that consumers may have built around
edge-case compatibility and bug-for-bug dependencies
semantic regression tests for documented and de facto behavior

signals: the implementation changes while the public signature stays the same
users depend on edge-case behavior or error handling
a refactor claims to be internal-only
performance, ordering, or retry semantics may shift

lenses.tension: Can preserve surprising legacy behavior and constrain internal simplification.

lenses.name: Compatibility Shim Containment
lenses.focus: Use adapters, translators, aliases, and emulation layers deliberately, with ownership and expiry, so compatibility support does not become permanent architecture.
concerns: adapter boundaries between old and new models
translation correctness for renamed, split, or merged concepts
feature flags and routing for legacy behavior
expiry dates and deletion criteria for shims
performance and observability of compatibility paths
preventing shim logic from leaking into core domain code

signals: the system must support old and new contracts at the same time
a clean internal model conflicts with legacy external behavior
migration requires aliases, redirects, or format translation
temporary compatibility code risks becoming permanent

lenses.tension: Preserves compatibility by adding code paths that can obscure the desired architecture and increase defect surface.

lenses.name: Contract Compatibility Harness
lenses.focus: Make compatibility mechanically testable through consumer contracts, golden fixtures, schema diffs, and cross-version integration runs.
concerns: consumer-driven contract coverage for critical clients
golden request, response, event, and error fixtures
old-client-to-new-server and new-client-to-old-server matrices
binary, wire, and serialization compatibility checks
contract test ownership and fixture update review
CI gates that distinguish intentional evolution from accidental breakage

signals: compatibility is asserted by convention rather than tests
multiple clients or services integrate through a stable boundary
serialization formats, generated clients, or SDKs are changing
a past incident involved a silent contract break

lenses.tension: Raises maintenance cost for fixtures and matrices, and can slow refactors when contracts were never explicitly modeled.

lenses.name: Deprecation Lifecycle Design
lenses.focus: Turn removals and behavior shifts into an explicit lifecycle with notices, telemetry, migration aids, deadlines, and enforced removal criteria.
concerns: deprecation announcement channels and minimum support windows
runtime warnings, compile-time annotations, and documentation markers
usage telemetry before removal
replacement APIs and automated migration guidance
sunset criteria, owner, and date tracking
policy for emergency deprecations and security-driven removals

signals: an old API, field, endpoint, behavior, or dependency needs to be removed
users have long-lived clients or slow upgrade cycles
the product lacks a documented support window
previous deprecations lingered forever or broke consumers abruptly

lenses.tension: Protects users at the cost of carrying old behavior, duplicate documentation, and removal bookkeeping.

lenses.name: Legacy Removal and Cleanup
lenses.focus: Make compatibility debt finite by planning safe removal, verification, and simplification after consumers have migrated.
concerns: explicit removal criteria tied to telemetry and support policy
cleanup sequencing after expand-contract migrations
dead code, dead schema, and dead configuration detection
major-version removal packaging
user-facing migration completion checks
post-removal monitoring for missed consumers

signals: deprecated paths, old fields, or compatibility shims have accumulated
maintenance cost is dominated by old versions or legacy behavior
a major release or contract cleanup is being planned
telemetry suggests migration is complete but cleanup is not scheduled

lenses.tension: Pushes toward simplification and removal, which can conflict with conservative compatibility instincts.

lenses.name: Expand-Contract Migration Safety
lenses.focus: Sequence migrations so each deployed step is independently safe, reversible where practical, and compatible with mixed old and new application versions.
concerns: expand, dual-read, dual-write, backfill, switch, and contract phases
rolling deploy and blue-green compatibility
idempotent backfills and resumable jobs
rollback behavior after partial migration
foreign key, uniqueness, and constraint timing
data verification before destructive cleanup

signals: database schema, persisted model, queue payload, or storage layout changes
deployment cannot stop all writers and readers at once
backfills or destructive cleanup are required
rollback requirements are unclear

lenses.tension: Often requires temporary duplication and phased work instead of a single clean refactor.

lenses.name: Migration Observability Gates
lenses.focus: Require migrations and compatibility transitions to expose adoption, correctness, lag, and rollback signals before advancing phases.
concerns: per-version and per-contract usage telemetry
backfill progress, error rate, and data parity metrics
dark reads, shadow comparisons, and sampled validation
phase-entry and phase-exit gates
kill switches and rollback runbooks
alerting for legacy-path traffic after expected sunset

signals: a phased migration depends on assumptions about client adoption or data parity
removal decisions lack usage data
rollback or kill-switch behavior needs proof
migration health cannot currently be observed in production

lenses.tension: Adds instrumentation and operational gates that can feel heavy for teams focused only on code completion.

lenses.name: Persisted Data Forward Compatibility
lenses.focus: Ensure durable data, logs, snapshots, caches, and serialized state remain readable and recoverable across code upgrades, rollbacks, and restores.
concerns: on-disk and stored-object format versioning
read-old-write-new and read-new-during-rollback behavior
event replay after schema or semantics changes
cache invalidation and mixed-format cache populations
backup restore compatibility across releases
irreversible writes and downgrade boundaries

signals: the change touches persisted state, event logs, snapshots, caches, or backups
rollback after deploy is required
old data may be replayed into new code
stored formats are undocumented or inferred from current structs

lenses.tension: May prohibit elegant format simplifications until a full migration and restore story exists.

lenses.name: Rapid Experimentation Isolation
lenses.focus: Give unstable ideas a contained path through flags, preview APIs, opt-in cohorts, and disposable contracts without weakening stable compatibility promises.
