concerns: preview, beta, and experimental contract labeling
feature flags and cohort isolation
temporary schemas and non-stable endpoints
promotion criteria from experiment to stable API
rollback and data cleanup for failed experiments
preventing experimental behavior from leaking into default paths

signals: the team needs fast iteration but also has stable consumers
new behavior is uncertain and may be withdrawn
experiments touch API shape, persisted data, or protocol behavior
users need opt-in access before long-term support is promised

lenses.tension: Optimizes learning speed while requiring strict containment so experiments do not become accidental stable contracts.

lenses.name: Rollout Blast Radius Control
lenses.focus: The catalog has migration and experimentation lenses, but not a general progressive rollout lens for canaries, cohorts, kill switches, rollback criteria, and exposure limits.

lenses.name: SemVer Release Governance
lenses.focus: Classify every user-visible change by compatibility impact and force release versioning, changelog, and rollout policy to match that classification.
concerns: breaking vs additive vs patch-level behavioral changes
public API and wire-contract diff gates in CI
major-version criteria and exception handling
changelog accuracy and upgrade notes
pre-release, experimental, and stable surface labeling
release cadence pressure that hides breaking changes

signals: the change modifies a published API, SDK, CLI, protocol, schema, or configuration format
teams disagree whether a change is breaking
the project has downstream consumers outside the immediate team
release notes or version bumps are currently manual and inconsistent

lenses.tension: Adds process and may delay small releases, especially when teams want to ship behavior changes without admitting a major-version boundary.

lenses.name: Version Negotiation Capabilities
lenses.focus: Design explicit negotiation of protocol versions and capabilities instead of assuming every participant upgrades in lockstep.
concerns: capability discovery and feature probing
minimum and maximum supported protocol versions
graceful fallback paths for unsupported features
client, server, broker, and peer compatibility matrices
error semantics for version mismatch
upgrade order for distributed deployments

signals: clients and servers are deployed independently
multiple protocol versions must coexist
peer-to-peer, plugin, extension, or agent ecosystems are involved
the design currently relies on global synchronized upgrades

lenses.tension: Increases protocol complexity and test matrix size, especially when capabilities interact combinatorially.

lenses.name: Characterization Safety
lenses.focus: Owns safe change to legacy or poorly understood behavior by pinning what exists before deciding what should change.
concerns: golden master and approval tests for broad existing behavior
bug reproduction tests that distinguish intentional fixes from accidental drift
sampling production-like inputs while scrubbing secrets and instability
snapshot scope limited to meaningful outputs instead of noisy implementation artifacts
planned replacement of characterization tests with clearer specifications after refactor

signals: legacy code has weak tests and unclear intended behavior
a migration or refactor must preserve behavior before changing design
stakeholders cannot fully specify all existing edge cases
regressions are more dangerous than temporarily accepting ugly tests

lenses.tension: Can preserve accidental behavior and create brittle snapshots unless paired with a plan to refine or delete tests after learning.

lenses.name: Feedback Economics
lenses.focus: Owns the shape of the test pyramid and CI workflow so the suite gives fast, trustworthy feedback at the right cost.
concerns: suite segmentation by unit, property, contract, integration, end-to-end, smoke, and soak tests
fast pre-commit and pull-request gates versus slower nightly or release gates
flake budgets, quarantine policy, and test runtime ownership
parallelization, caching, sharding, and selective execution without hiding risk
failure output that points to the broken behavior quickly

signals: CI is too slow for developers to trust or wait for
expensive tests run on every trivial change while high-risk checks run too late
the suite has many flaky or quarantined tests without ownership
a program needs a release gate strategy across many packages or services

lenses.tension: Optimizes system-level confidence and developer throughput, but may defer some exhaustive checks away from the fastest path.

lenses.name: Boundary Contracts
lenses.focus: Owns executable contracts at API, port, schema, and integration boundaries so producers and consumers cannot silently drift.
concerns: consumer-driven contracts for external APIs and internal service boundaries
provider verification against examples, schemas, status codes, and error semantics
backward-compatible request and response evolution
port contract suites reusable across real adapters, fakes, and test doubles
serialization, pagination, idempotency, retries, and timeout behavior

signals: multiple teams, services, plugins, or adapters depend on the same boundary
mocked tests pass while real integrations fail
schemas, events, or API responses evolve independently from consumers
a library promises adapter compatibility across implementations

lenses.tension: Adds maintenance overhead to boundary evolution and can slow iteration when contracts are introduced before the interaction is stable.

lenses.name: Coverage That Matters
lenses.focus: Owns coverage as a risk map of important behavior, not a vanity percentage of executed lines.
concerns: critical paths, failure modes, permissions, data loss, and money-moving behavior
mutation testing or fault injection for high-risk logic
branch and condition coverage where decisions matter
untested negative paths and recovery paths
coverage thresholds scoped by module risk instead of global mandates

signals: coverage percentage is high but defects still escape
a team is debating where to add tests before release
tests execute code without meaningful assertions
high-risk modules have the same coverage expectations as low-risk glue

lenses.tension: Rejects easy numeric targets and may produce uncomfortable gaps that require prioritization instead of broad shallow test additions.

lenses.name: Hermetic Determinism
lenses.focus: Owns removal of flakiness by making tests isolated, deterministic, parallel-safe, and independent of wall-clock or environment state.
concerns: controlled clocks, seeded randomness, deterministic schedulers, and explicit timeouts
test isolation for databases, file systems, queues, caches, environment variables, and ports
parallel execution without shared mutable fixtures or order dependence
no sleeps as synchronization; use observable readiness and eventual assertions with bounds
repeatable local, CI, and containerized execution

signals: tests fail intermittently in CI or only under parallel execution
suite relies on real time, random data, shared services, or execution order
developers rerun failed tests until they pass
a change introduces async, concurrency, scheduling, or background workers

lenses.tension: May require deeper control points and stricter environment setup than teams expect, especially around concurrency and external services.

lenses.name: Critical Path Integration
lenses.focus: Owns a small, deliberate set of integration and end-to-end tests that prove real wiring, contracts, persistence, and deployment assumptions.
concerns: happy path and top failure path for each business-critical workflow
real adapter wiring, migrations, serialization, auth, permissions, and persistence
environment parity for containers, config, secrets, and service discovery
thin end-to-end smoke tests backed by broader lower-level coverage
clear ownership of slow tests, quarantines, and release gates

signals: unit tests pass but deployed workflows break
risk comes from wiring, configuration, database migrations, or cross-service behavior
release confidence depends on a few user-critical flows
the team has either no end-to-end tests or a bloated slow suite

lenses.tension: Consumes runtime and operational complexity, so it must stay selective and should not replace faster unit, property, or contract tests.

lenses.name: Property Invariants
lenses.focus: Owns the discovery and encoding of invariants so broad input spaces are tested instead of a few hand-picked examples.
concerns: algebraic properties such as idempotence, commutativity, reversibility, monotonicity, and conservation
state-machine transitions and illegal-state rejection
round-trip properties for parsers, serializers, mappers, and codecs
shrinking-friendly generators that produce valid domain data
metamorphic tests for behavior without a perfect oracle

signals: logic has many edge cases, combinations, or input ranges
examples keep missing bugs in parsers, calculators, reconciliation, permissions, or state transitions
the system transforms data and should preserve clear invariants
production defects cluster around unexpected input shapes

lenses.tension: Requires sharper domain modeling and generator discipline; it can waste effort when behavior is mostly simple wiring or presentation.

lenses.name: Red Green Refactor
lenses.focus: Owns the discipline of driving small behavior slices from failing tests before implementation and refactoring only under a green suite.
concerns: first failing test expresses user-visible or domain-visible behavior
minimal implementation avoids speculative abstraction
refactor phase improves names, boundaries, and duplication without changing behavior
tests assert outcomes rather than incidental call order
small commits preserve a clear behavioral trail

signals: requirements are concrete enough to express as examples
implementation details are tempting but the desired behavior is still small
a bug fix needs a reproducing test before code changes
the team wants tighter feedback during a risky refactor

lenses.tension: Optimizes local feedback and design pressure, but can be too granular for exploratory spikes, broad migrations, or unknown integration behavior.

lenses.name: Test Data Discipline
lenses.focus: Owns fixture, builder, factory, and generator design so tests are readable, valid by default, and resilient to model evolution.
concerns: domain builders that name intent instead of exposing every field
valid-by-default fixtures with explicit overrides for edge cases
minimal data per test to avoid hidden coupling and irrelevant assertions
shared generators for property tests, contract tests, and integration tests
avoidance of brittle snapshots and copy-pasted fixture blobs

signals: tests are hard to read because setup is noisy or copied everywhere
schema or domain model changes break many unrelated tests
invalid fixtures accidentally test impossible states
property and contract tests need reusable realistic data generation

lenses.tension: Adds a small abstraction layer to tests and can hide important data details if builders become too magical.

lenses.name: Honest Test Doubles
lenses.focus: Owns the choice of fakes, stubs, mocks, spies, and simulators so tests isolate intentionally without lying about production behavior.
concerns: fakes verified by the same contract suite as real adapters
mocks limited to externally observable collaboration, not internal choreography
stubs that model important errors, latency, pagination, retries, and partial failures
in-memory substitutes that do not hide transaction, ordering, or consistency semantics
removal of brittle over-specified interaction tests

signals: tests pass with mocks but fail with real dependencies
mock setup is longer than the behavior being tested
in-memory fake behavior diverges from real storage or messaging semantics
test suites frequently break after harmless implementation refactors

lenses.tension: Favors fewer and more truthful doubles, which can increase setup cost and push some tests toward slower integration coverage.

lenses.name: Testable Seams
lenses.focus: Owns the placement of seams so core behavior can be tested deterministically without dragging infrastructure into every test.
concerns: pure domain functions separated from I/O orchestration
explicit ports for time, randomness, storage, network, identity, and external services
dependency direction that lets tests substitute adapters without reflection or global state
transaction and async boundaries that are observable without sleeping or polling
public behavior seams instead of testing private implementation details

signals: tests require real databases, queues, file systems, clocks, or network calls for ordinary business rules
code uses globals, singletons, static calls, ambient context, or hard-coded constructors
unit tests are slow because domain logic is tangled with framework lifecycle
a refactor needs confidence but the current design has no clean injection points

lenses.tension: Pushes for explicit boundaries and dependency injection, which can add indirection and feel heavy for small one-off code paths.
